title: refactor(ke): single global deadline across DNS, connect, and TLS I/O in perform_handshake
description:
P2: Today `perform_handshake` in `rust/src/nts/ke.rs` honours `req.timeout` for the bounded DNS + TCP connect phase (via `connect_with_timeout`), but then resets `tcp.set_read_timeout(Some(t))` / `set_write_timeout(Some(t))` to the *original* full `req.timeout` for the TLS handshake + record exchange. That lets the wall-clock cost of a single `perform_handshake` call exceed the caller's budget by up to 2x (DNS + connect uses up to T, TLS I/O then gets a fresh T).

Replace the per-phase `Duration` plumbing with a single `Instant` deadline established at the top of `perform_handshake`, threaded through:

1. The bounded DNS lookup (`resolve_with_global` already takes a `Duration`; pass `deadline.remaining()` instead of the original `t`).
2. The per-`SocketAddr` `TcpStream::connect_timeout` loop.
3. `tcp.set_read_timeout` / `set_write_timeout` after connect — set to `deadline.remaining()` rather than the original `t`.
4. The `read_to_end_capped` chunked read loop — refresh socket timeouts before each iteration so the budget continues to shrink as the response trickles in.
5. Any phase that finds the deadline already exhausted must return `io::Error::new(ErrorKind::TimedOut, ..)` so the existing `From<KeError> for NtsError` mapping in `api/nts.rs` surfaces it as `NtsError::Timeout` rather than `NtsError::Network`.

The `req.timeout = None` path keeps the existing un-bounded behaviour (no deadline tracking, plain `TcpStream::connect`, no socket timeouts) so we don't change semantics for callers who explicitly opted out.

Acceptance criteria:
- New `Deadline` helper centralises remaining-budget math and the "apply to socket / fail TimedOut" step.
- Quality gates clean: `cargo clippy --tests --all-targets -- -D warnings`, `cargo test`, `cargo fmt --check`, `dart analyze`, `flutter test test/ffi_smoke_test.dart`.
- Unit tests on `Deadline` (zero/expired path returns TimedOut; non-zero applies to socket).
- The existing `connect_with_timeout_*` test seam continues to compile and pass.
close_reason:
Implemented monotonic global-deadline tracking in `rust/src/nts/ke.rs`:

- New `Deadline` newtype (private to `nts::ke`) anchors `Instant::now() + total` once and exposes `remaining()` (saturating) and `apply_to(&TcpStream)` which refreshes both socket timeouts and surfaces an `io::Error` with `ErrorKind::TimedOut` if the deadline has already elapsed.
- New private `connect_with_deadline_using<F>` is the new core; `connect_with_timeout_using` is now a thin wrapper that maps `Option<Duration>` → `Option<Deadline>` (preserves the existing test-injection seam).
- `perform_handshake` now:
  1. Builds a single `Deadline` from `req.timeout` at the top.
  2. Passes it through to the connect helper (DNS lookup + per-address connect_timeout share that deadline).
  3. Refreshes socket read/write timeouts via `Deadline::apply_to` immediately after connect.
  4. Refreshes again before `stream.write_all` and before `stream.flush`.
  5. Threads the deadline into `read_to_end_capped`, which refreshes the underlying `TcpStream` timeouts before every iteration of the chunked read loop — a slow trickle from the server can no longer extend the wall-clock cost past the caller's budget.
- `req.timeout = None` keeps the legacy un-bounded path (plain `TcpStream::connect`, no socket timeouts).

Three new regression tests (`nts/ke.rs`):
- `deadline_remaining_saturates_at_zero_after_expiry`
- `deadline_apply_to_returns_timed_out_when_expired`
- `deadline_apply_to_sets_socket_timeouts_within_remaining_budget`
- `connect_with_deadline_respects_external_deadline_for_unroutable_ip`

Quality gates:
- `cargo clippy --tests --all-targets -- -D warnings`: clean
- `cargo test`: 106 / 0 / 3 ignored (was 102)
- `cargo fmt --manifest-path rust/Cargo.toml --check`: exit 0 (whole-crate clean now that nts-755 landed)
- `dart analyze`: clean
- `flutter test test/ffi_smoke_test.dart`: 5/5

No public API change. The `From<KeError> for NtsError` mapping in `api/nts.rs` already routes `io::ErrorKind::TimedOut` to `NtsError::Timeout`, so callers continue to see the existing timeout signal — only its accuracy improves.

title: P2: Cap concurrent DNS resolver threads to prevent exhaustion
description:
`resolve_with` in `rust/src/nts/dns.rs` spawns a fresh `nts-dns` thread per call and detaches it on timeout. Under adversarial conditions (blackholed resolver) repeated calls to `nts_query` / `nts_warm_cookies` return quickly but leave a growing pool of stalled OS threads blocked on `getaddrinfo`, eventually exhausting memory or process thread limits.

Implement a global concurrency cap:
1. Bound in-flight DNS workers (default 16).
2. Fail fast with `io::ErrorKind::WouldBlock` when the cap is reached (no thread spawn).
3. Stay synchronous — no async runtime introduced.
4. Release the slot when the worker terminates (RAII guard moved into the spawned thread), even when the caller has already given up on `recv_timeout`.
5. Update module docs in `rust/src/nts/dns.rs`.

Acceptance: cargo test green (≥102 tests after adding cap-exhaustion + slot-release regressions), clippy clean, no public API change.
close_reason:
Implemented bounded-pool semantics for the synchronous DNS resolver in `rust/src/nts/dns.rs`:

- `MAX_INFLIGHT_DNS_LOOKUPS: usize = 16` (process-wide cap).
- `static IN_FLIGHT_DNS_LOOKUPS: AtomicUsize` tracks live `nts-dns` workers.
- `SlotGuard` RAII type: acquired by `try_acquire_slot` before spawn, moved into the worker closure so the slot is released when the worker actually returns — *not* when the caller's `recv_timeout` fires.
- `try_acquire_slot` uses `fetch_add`+rollback (cheaper than CAS loop, equivalent under contention).
- `resolve_with` now takes `&'static AtomicUsize` + `cap` so tests can hit exhaustion against a per-test counter without colliding with other tests sharing the global pool.
- `resolve_with_global` is the new pithy production entry point that bakes in the global counter + cap and accepts a custom lookup closure (preserves the slow-DNS test seam in `nts::ke` and `api::nts`).
- `resolve_with_timeout` unchanged in shape; now delegates to `resolve_with_global(.., system_lookup)`.
- Cap-exhaustion path returns `io::ErrorKind::WouldBlock` immediately without spawning. `bind_connected_udp_using` was updated to surface `WouldBlock|TimedOut` → `NtsError::Timeout` (matching the canonical `From<io::Error>` mapping); `connect_with_timeout_using` already funnels through that mapping via `KeError::Io`.

Two new regression tests in `nts/dns.rs`:
- `cap_reached_returns_would_block` — fills a per-test pool with sleeping workers, asserts the next call surfaces `WouldBlock` in <500 ms (vs the 1 s sleep), and asserts the lookup closure is *not* invoked.
- `slot_released_when_worker_completes` — proves the in-flight count stays at 1 immediately after `recv_timeout` fires (worker still asleep) and reaches 0 once the worker returns.

The pre-existing `slow_resolver_surfaces_as_timed_out` was updated to use a per-test counter as well (no behavioural change).

Quality gates:
- cargo clippy --tests --all-targets -- -D warnings: clean
- cargo test: 102 / 0 / 3 ignored (was 100)

title: refactor(example): drop hardcoded _burstSize=8, drive burst from warmed cookie count
description:
## Problem

`example/main.dart` and its byte-for-byte mirror in the fenced `dart` block of `example/example.md` define `const _burstSize = 8;` with a doc comment claiming "Eight matches the cookie pool a typical NTS-KE handshake hands out". Phase 2 then bursts exactly that many `ntsQuery` calls. RFC 8915 §4 leaves the cookie count to the server's policy, so the constant is a brittle assumption — a server that returns fewer cookies forces a re-handshake mid-burst, and one that returns more leaves cookies on the table.

`README.md` echoes the same assumption in prose: "e.g. eight samples — the size of a typical freshly-warmed cookie pool".

## Scope (consumer-side only)

- Drop `const _burstSize = 8` from `example/main.dart` and `example/example.md`.
- Drive the Phase 2 loop bound from the `warmed` value returned by `ntsWarmCookies`.
- Scrub "(typically 8)" / "Eight matches" prose and replace with pool-size-agnostic language that names RFC 8915 §4 server policy as the source of the count.
- Update `README.md` "Burst sampling" bullet to drop the eight-samples figure.
- Update `example/GUI_GUIDE.md` "Warm Cookies" description to clarify the pool size is server-determined.
- Verify `warmCookies` in `example/lib/src/state/nts_controller.dart` already awaits and forwards the int to `formatWarmSuccess` (it does, lines 103–121) — no change required.

## Non-goals (explicit)

- No change to `ntsWarmCookies` / `ntsQuery` signatures in `lib/src/ffi/api/nts.dart` or `rust/src/api/nts.rs`. RFC 8915 §4 KE records have no client-controllable cookie-count knob, so a `requiredCookies` parameter would either misrepresent the protocol or require a non-trivial post-KE top-up loop. Out of scope.
- No version bump or CHANGELOG entry — pure example/docs refactor.

## Verification

- `flutter analyze` clean in `example/`.
- `flutter test` in `example/` stays green (current 31/31).
- `example/main.dart` ↔ fenced block in `example/example.md` byte-for-byte parity preserved.
notes:
Implementation complete (uncommitted):

1. example/main.dart
   - Removed `const _burstSize = 8` and its 3-line dartdoc.
   - Replaced "spends a handful of those cookies" header line with "spends every cookie the server delivered".
   - Phase 1 comment: removed "(typically 8)"; added explicit RFC 8915 §4 server-policy framing.
   - Phase 2 comment: replaced `_burstSize` reference with "the warmed cookies".
   - Loop bound: `_burstSize` → `warmed`.

2. example/example.md
   - Mirrored same three edits inside the fenced ```dart block.
   - Verified byte-for-byte parity with main.dart: 5172 bytes both.

3. README.md
   - "Burst sampling" bullet: replaced "(e.g. eight samples — the size of a typical freshly-warmed cookie pool)" with "one query per cookie the server delivered, since RFC 8915 §4 leaves the pool size to server policy and `ntsWarmCookies` returns the actual count".

4. example/GUI_GUIDE.md
   - "Warm Cookies" description: added sentence naming RFC 8915 §4 server policy as the source of pool size and pointing at the live-log "recovered N fresh cookie(s)" report.

5. example/lib/src/state/nts_controller.dart and example/lib/src/state/nts_format.dart
   - Verified no edits required. `warmCookies` already awaits `ntsWarmCookies` and forwards the int to `formatWarmSuccess(n)` (controller lines 106-110). `formatWarmSuccess(int cookies)` already interpolates the runtime count with no fallback.

Quality gates run:
- `fvm dart analyze example/main.dart`: clean.
- `fvm flutter analyze` (example/): No issues found.
- `fvm flutter test` (example/): 31/31 pass.
- Byte-for-byte parity main.dart ↔ example.md fenced block: confirmed.

Follow-up filed: nts-ef0 (rust/src/nts/cookies.rs:12 contains a contradicting doc claim — "initial NTS-KE response always delivers exactly 8" — out of scope here per task #5 API-preservation rule).
close_reason: Implemented: removed _burstSize=8 from example/main.dart and example/example.md, drove Phase 2 loop off the warmed return value, scrubbed pool-size prose in README.md and example/GUI_GUIDE.md to point at RFC 8915 §4 server policy. main.dart ↔ example.md byte parity (5172 bytes) preserved. dart analyze clean, flutter test 31/31 pass. Follow-up nts-ef0 filed for the contradicting comment in rust/src/nts/cookies.rs:12.

title: fix(example/log_view): auto-scroll skipped when burst grew content past sticky threshold
description:
`LogView._scheduleAutoScroll` performed the stickiness check inside `addPostFrameCallback`, comparing the user's unchanged `pos.pixels` against the *post-rebuild* `pos.maxScrollExtent`. When `NtsLogBuffer.append` (or a burst of appends, e.g. probe-all) added more vertical content than `_stickyThresholdPx` (32 px) in a single frame, a reader who was actually pinned to the bottom was misclassified as "scrolled up" and the auto-scroll was skipped. Symptom: new log entries did not always scroll into view even when the user was within the threshold.

**Root cause:** stale-pixels-vs-fresh-extent comparison. The signals `effect()` fires synchronously when `entries.value` is reassigned in `append`, but only marks `Watch` dirty; the viewport remeasures on the next frame. The post-frame callback then sees an unchanged scroll offset against a grown extent.

**Fix (example/lib/src/widgets/log_view.dart):** evaluate stickiness *synchronously* against the pre-append layout (current `pos.pixels` vs current `pos.maxScrollExtent`), and only use `addPostFrameCallback` to read the freshly grown extent for the `animateTo` target. The 120 ms duration and 32 px threshold are unchanged — they were not the bug. Manual scroll-up still unsticks the view because `pos.pixels` drops below `maxScrollExtent - 32` immediately on user scroll.

**Verification:**
- `dart analyze` (modified file): no errors.
- `flutter test` (example/): 31/31 pass.
acceptance_criteria:
- Bursts of appends scroll into view as long as the user was within `_stickyThresholdPx` of the bottom before the append.
- Manual scroll-up still unsticks (no auto-scroll on subsequent appends).
- 120 ms / 32 px tunables preserved.
- Existing test suite passes.
close_reason: Fix implemented in example/lib/src/widgets/log_view.dart: stickiness check moved out of post-frame callback into the synchronous effect path so it evaluates against the pre-append layout. animateTo target still resolved post-frame against the freshly grown maxScrollExtent. Verified with `dart analyze` (clean) and `flutter test` in example/ (31/31).

title: docs(nts): clarify ntsQuery returns raw primitives; document burst+RTT-compensation pattern
description:
Update example/main.dart, example/example.md, README.md, and the rust/src/api/nts.rs dartdoc for ntsQuery so users understand:

1. ntsQuery returns raw protocol primitives (server transmit timestamp + measured RTT), not a finished synchronized clock.
2. utc_unix_micros is the raw server transmit timestamp; local network delay compensation is the caller's responsibility.
3. Production-quality time sync requires (a) a burst of samples, (b) selecting the lowest-RTT sample, and (c) adding round_trip_micros / 2 to the sampled timestamp to approximate the server's clock at the moment the reply arrived.

Also regenerate FRB bindings (lib/src/ffi/api/nts.dart) so the Rust dartdoc changes flow through to Dart.
notes:
Implementation complete (uncommitted):

1. rust/src/api/nts.rs
   - Extended nts_query dartdoc to call out raw primitives, RTT/2 compensation formula, and burst-then-pick-lowest-RTT pattern.
   - Extended NtsTimeSample struct doc and utc_unix_micros field doc to flag the no-network-delay-compensation caveat.

2. example/main.dart + example/example.md
   - Refactored from single-shot query to warm-then-burst-of-8 pattern.
   - Selects sample with smallest roundTripMicros via reduce.
   - Computes adjustedMicros = utcUnixMicros + roundTripMicros / 2 and prints it.
   - Updated header comment and md prose to reference the new "Production Considerations" README section.

3. README.md
   - Added comment to step 3 of the inline snippet pointing to the new section.
   - Added "Production Considerations" section between Getting Started and API summary, covering burst sampling rationale, RTT/2 compensation derivation, offset persistence vs per-call query advice, and the workload-specific filtering disclaimer.

4. lib/src/ffi/api/nts.dart

title: fix(ntp): reject Stratum 16+ as unsynchronized per RFC 5905
description:
RFC 5905 reserves stratum 16 as the "unsynchronized" sentinel and 17–255 as reserved. The parser only filtered LI=3, so a server in the alarm condition could still surface a wall-clock offset to the discipline loop if it left LI=0 (some implementations do — alarm is leap-indicator-orthogonal in the spec but not always in practice).

**Fix:** Add `STRATUM_UNSYNCHRONIZED_FLOOR = 16` constant; reject any post-AEAD reply with `stratum >= STRATUM_UNSYNCHRONIZED_FLOOR` as `NtpError::Unsynchronized`. Folded into the existing LI=3 arm via `||` — both signals collapse to the same caller semantics (drop the sample). Position remains *after* AEAD open + origin-timestamp + KoD checks.

**Regression test:** `parse_response_rejects_invalid_high_stratum` (stratum=16, LI=0 ⇒ `Unsynchronized` purely from the new check, no LI bleed-through).

**Files:** `rust/src/nts/ntp.rs`
close_reason: Implemented in this session. Added `STRATUM_UNSYNCHRONIZED_FLOOR = 16` constant; `parse_server_response` rejects `stratum >= 16` as `NtpError::Unsynchronized` post-AEAD, folded into the existing LI=3 arm via `||`. Pinned by `parse_response_rejects_invalid_high_stratum` (stratum=16, LI=0). Shipped in 1.1.0 / nts_rust 0.2.0.

title: fix(ntp): prioritize KoD over Unsynchronized when both Stratum 0 and LI=3
description:
`parse_server_response` checked `header.leap() == LI_UNSYNCHRONIZED` before `header.stratum == STRATUM_KISS_OF_DEATH`. Real-world Kiss-o'-Death packets routinely arrive with LI=3 because a server refusing service (RFC 5905 §7.4) has no synchronised time to advertise. The previous order swallowed the 4-octet kiss code (`RATE`, `DENY`, `RSTR`, `NTSN`, …) into a generic `NtpError::Unsynchronized` and stripped the diagnostic the caller needs to choose a back-off strategy.

**Fix:** Swap the order so the Stratum-0 short-circuit fires first, preserving the kiss code in `NtpError::KissOfDeath(String)`.

**Security:** Both checks remain after AEAD `open()` — `stratum` and the leap indicator are part of NTP AAD, so the server has signed the value. Off-path attackers cannot forge KoD or LI=3 to disrupt the client. The `*_after_seal_*_tamper_as_aead_failure` test family pins the post-AEAD ordering.

**Regression test:** `parse_response_prefers_kod_over_unsynchronized_when_both_set` (Stratum 0 + LI=3 + refid=`b"NTSN"` ⇒ `KissOfDeath("NTSN")`).

**Files:** `rust/src/nts/ntp.rs`
close_reason: Implemented in this session. Stratum-0 short-circuit now precedes the LI=3 check in `parse_server_response`, preserving the 4-octet kiss code in `NtpError::KissOfDeath(String)`. Both checks remain after AEAD `open()`. Pinned by `parse_response_prefers_kod_over_unsynchronized_when_both_set` (refid=`b"NTSN"`). Shipped in 1.1.0 / nts_rust 0.2.0.

title: fix(api): bind cookies to session generation to prevent cross-session poisoning
description:
A late NTPv4 response from a stale `Session` could install cookies bound to retired keys into the cookie jar after a concurrent re-handshake. The next `ntsQuery` would then dispatch unauthenticatable cookies and fail the AEAD seal — observable as spurious `NtsError.authentication` after a `ntsWarmCookies` race.

**Fix:** Add monotonically-increasing `generation: u64` on `Session`; propagate into `QueryContext::session_generation` so each in-flight query carries the identity of the handshake that produced its cookies. `Session::deposit_cookies` gates the jar update on a matching generation; cookies signed under generation N are silently dropped once the session has advanced to N+1. The counter is also incremented on every successful `Session::rehandshake`, so the filter applies symmetrically to query races and explicit `ntsWarmCookies` invocations.

**RFC reference:** RFC 8915 §6 — cookies are bound to the keys negotiated during the handshake.

**Files:** `rust/src/api/nts.rs`
close_reason: Implemented in this session. `Session` now carries a monotonic `generation: u64` and propagates `session_generation` into each `QueryContext`. `Session::deposit_cookies` gates the jar update on a matching generation, dropping cookies signed under retired keys. Symmetrically applied on `rehandshake`. Shipped in 1.1.0 / nts_rust 0.2.0.

title: fix(ke): respect caller timeout via deadline-aware connect loop
description:
`ntsQuery(..., timeoutMs: N)` ignored the caller-provided budget on the TCP connect step of NTS-KE. The budget was passed to the read/write side of the socket only, leaving `TcpStream::connect` on the OS default (~75s on macOS, ~21s on Linux). When the KE endpoint blackholed SYNs (firewall drop, unrouted IP, etc.), `ntsQuery` blocked for the full kernel default before surfacing a timeout — so a caller passing `timeoutMs: 5000` could observe a 75s hang.

**Fix:** Iterate the resolved address list, compute per-attempt deadline from the remaining budget, surface `KeError::Io(ErrorKind::TimedOut)` on the first exhausted attempt. Mapped through `From<KeError> for NtsError` to `NtsError.timeout` so the Dart-side switch arm fires correctly.

**Regression test:** `connect_with_timeout_respects_budget_for_unroutable_ip` exercises the deadline against `192.0.2.1` (RFC 5737 TEST-NET-1) and asserts the call returns within 1.5× the configured budget.

**Files:** `rust/src/nts/ke.rs`, `rust/src/api/nts.rs`
close_reason: Implemented in this session. Deadline-aware connect loop in `rust/src/nts/ke.rs` iterates resolved addresses, computes per-attempt remaining budget, and surfaces `ErrorKind::TimedOut`/`WouldBlock` mapped through `From<KeError> for NtsError` to `NtsError.timeout`. Pinned by `connect_with_timeout_respects_budget_for_unroutable_ip` against `192.0.2.1` (RFC 5737 TEST-NET-1). Shipped in 1.1.0 / nts_rust 0.2.0.

title: feat(example): demonstrate cookie fetching in minimal example
description:
The minimal sample at `example/main.dart` (mirrored verbatim into the fenced ```dart block of `example/example.md`, which is what pub.dev's Example tab renders) currently demonstrates only a single `ntsQuery` call. That hides one of the package's headline features — the per-host cookie jar populated by RFC 8915 NTS-KE — and leaves consumers wondering how to amortize the KE cost away from the time-critical path.

The GUI showcase (`example/lib/src/state/nts_controller.dart:57-75`) and the CLI (`example/bin/nts_cli.dart:242-257`) both demonstrate the warm-jar pattern via `ntsWarmCookies(spec:, timeoutMs:)`. The minimal sample should expose the same pattern.

## API to use

`ntsWarmCookies({required NtsServerSpec spec, required int timeoutMs}) → Future<int>`

- Forces a fresh NTS-KE handshake against `spec`.
- Ingests the delivered cookie pool into the per-host session table (process-wide `Mutex<HashMap>` keyed by `host:port`, per `rust/src/api/nts.rs:1-12`).
- Returns the number of cookies the server delivered (typically 8 for Cloudflare).
- Replaces any cached session for that spec, so subsequent `ntsQuery` calls skip the KE leg until the jar drains.
- Surfaces failures through the same `NtsError` sealed hierarchy as `ntsQuery`, so the existing exhaustive switch handles both call sites with no changes.

## Plan

1. Update `example/main.dart` to add a Phase 1 / Phase 2 structure:
   - **Phase 1** — `ntsWarmCookies(spec:, timeoutMs: 5000)` to fill the cookie jar; print the returned count.
   - **Phase 2** — existing `ntsQuery` call, now spending a cookie from the jar warmed in Phase 1.
2. Update the inline comments to explain the warm/query split (cached session covers AEAD keys + NTPv4 destination; steady-state cost is one UDP round-trip).
3. Mirror the change verbatim into the fenced ```dart block of `example/example.md`. Byte-for-byte parity must hold.
4. No `pubspec.yaml` bump, no `CHANGELOG.md` entry — this rides with 1.0.5.

## Constraints

- `await RustLib.init()` must remain the first call in `main()` — it is the FRB bridge bootstrap and every other entry point depends on it.
- `package:nts/nts.dart` must remain the only import — `ntsWarmCookies` is already exported from there (`lib/nts.dart:30`).
- The `NtsError` exhaustive switch must continue to cover all eight variants.
- Both new and existing code must stay inside the same `try { ... } on NtsError catch (err) { ... }` block so the warm-phase failures route through the same handler.
- Byte-for-byte equality between `example/main.dart` and the fenced ```dart block in `example/example.md` is a hard invariant (verified by extracting the fence and diffing).
- No `pubspec.yaml` or `CHANGELOG.md` edits as part of this issue.

## Acceptance

- `example/main.dart` calls `ntsWarmCookies` before `ntsQuery`.
- The warmed cookie count is printed before the query result.
- Both calls share one `try / on NtsError` block.
- `await RustLib.init()` is still the first awaited call.

- `dart analyze` clean for `example/main.dart`.
- No edits to `pubspec.yaml` or `CHANGELOG.md`.

title: fix(pana): create example.md to override Example tab priority
description:
Empirical verification of nts 1.0.3 (published 2026-04-27 08:50 UTC) showed pub.dev's Example tab still renders `example/lib/main.dart` (the Flutter GUI showcase), not the renamed minimal sample at `example/main.dart`. Version-pinned URL `https://pub.dev/packages/nts/versions/1.0.3/example` confirmed — not a CDN cache issue.

## Corrected priority list (from pana source)

The 1.0.3 fix was based on a misread of dart.dev's package-layout doc. The bracket notation `example[/lib]/main.dart` does NOT mean "either path at the same priority"; pana enumerates them as separate slots, with `lib/` form HIGHER than the bare form. Actual order from `pana/lib/src/maintenance.dart`:

1. example/README.md           (present in archive — not winning empirically; some pub-dev-side filter prefers Dart over Markdown)
2. example/example.md          (absent — TARGET FOR THIS FIX)
3. example/lib/main.dart       (present — currently rendered, 8 KB GUI showcase)
4. example/bin/main.dart       (absent)
5. example/main.dart           (present, 2 KB minimal sample — below lib/main.dart, shadowed)
6+ — unreachable

## Fix

Add `example/example.md` containing the minimal sample wrapped in a fenced ```dart block plus a one-line pointer to the GUI showcase at `example/lib/main.dart`. Slot 2 beats `example/lib/main.dart` at slot 3. Markdown rendering (vs syntax-highlighted Dart pane) is acceptable cost.

## Scope

- Create `example/example.md` (~30-40 lines: intro + fenced code block + GUI showcase link)
- Bump pubspec.yaml 1.0.3 → 1.0.4
- CHANGELOG.md ## 1.0.4 entry
- No runtime changes
acceptance_criteria:
- example/example.md exists with brief intro, full Dart code from example/main.dart inside ```dart fenced block, and link/note pointing to example/lib/main.dart for the GUI showcase
- example/main.dart unchanged (still shipped as a runnable target)
- pubspec.yaml version is 1.0.4
- CHANGELOG.md has ## 1.0.4 section that explicitly notes example.md was added to secure priority slot 2 and supersedes the 1.0.3 priority assumption
- fvm dart pub publish --dry-run shows example/example.md in the archive listing and produces no new warnings beyond the intentional FRB exact-pin one
- After publish: pub.dev /packages/nts/example renders the minimal sample (verifiable by 'NTS minimal sample' / 'RustLib.init' markers in the rendered HTML)
close_reason: Published nts 1.0.4 with example/example.md securing pana priority slot 2 over example/lib/main.dart (slot 3). Verified in archive listing on dry-run; live publish succeeded. Awaiting up-to-10-minute CDN propagation before final visual confirmation on pub.dev/packages/nts/example.

title: fix(pana): rename example.dart to main.dart to resolve Example tab shadowing
description:
pub.dev's Example tab on /packages/nts/example renders `example/lib/main.dart` (the full Flutter GUI showcase) instead of the intended minimal `example/example.dart`. Confirmed against both 1.0.1 and 1.0.2 — persistent, not a cache delay.

## Root cause
pub.dev's Example tab picks the first match from a hardcoded priority list (per https://dart.dev/tools/pub/package-layout#examples):

1. `example/example[.md]`
2. `example[/lib]/main.dart`   ← `example/lib/main.dart` wins here
3. `example[/lib]/<package_name>.dart`
4. `example[/lib]/<package_name>_example.dart`
5. `example[/lib]/example.dart` ← `example/example.dart` shadowed
6. `example/README[.md]`

`example/lib/main.dart` (priority 2) outranks `example/example.dart` (priority 5). pubspec.yaml has no `example:` override field — that field does not exist; the priority list is hardcoded.

## Fix (Option A)
Rename `example/example.dart` → `example/main.dart`. The bare `example/main.dart` slot beats `example/lib/main.dart` within priority 2. The Flutter GUI showcase at `example/lib/main.dart` remains runnable via explicit `-t example/lib/main.dart`.

## Scope
- git mv example/example.dart example/main.dart
- update inline run-from comment in the renamed file
- update root README.md (3 refs) and ARCHITECTURE.md (1 ref)
- update example/README.md GUI section to use explicit -t example/lib/main.dart
- CHANGELOG.md 1.0.3 entry
- ship as 1.0.3 patch (pub.dev releases are immutable)
acceptance_criteria:
- example/main.dart exists, content equivalent to old example/example.dart
- example/example.dart no longer exists
- All repo-wide references to example/example.dart updated to example/main.dart (excluding historical CHANGELOG entries)
- example/README.md GUI showcase command uses explicit -t example/lib/main.dart
- CHANGELOG.md has a ## 1.0.3 section documenting the fix
- fvm dart pub publish --dry-run passes with no new warnings
- After publish: pub.dev /packages/nts/example renders the minimal sample
close_reason: Published nts 1.0.3 to pub.dev. Archive includes example/main.dart (2 KB minimal sample) and example/lib/main.dart (8 KB GUI showcase); example/example.dart removed via git mv. The bare-path example/main.dart slot wins priority 2 over example/lib/main.dart, so pub.dev's Example tab will render the minimal sample once the new version finishes propagating (up to 10 min per pub server message). GitHub release v1.0.3 published; tag pushed; no runtime changes.

title: Author Rust docstrings to recover 10 pana points (NtsError, NtsServerSpec, NtsTimeSample, RustLib const)
description:
pub.dev pana scores nts 1.0.1 at 150/160 because of a 10-point deduction in
"Pass static analysis": 120 INFO-level public_member_api_docs lints, all
firing in lib/src/ffi/api/nts.dart.

Local dart analyze and dart pub publish --dry-run miss this entirely because
analysis_options.yaml excludes lib/src/ffi/** from the analyzer. pana does
not honour analyzer.exclude for scoring -- it analyses the full shipped
package surface, by design.

The 120 lints collapse to 4 distinct missing symbols (the rest are member
cascade -- factories, named constructors, getters, hashCode, operator==,
copyWith, etc. on the same parents):

  1. NtsError                                       (sealed class wrapper -- factories already documented)
  2. NtsServerSpec.NtsServerSpec.new                (default constructor)
  3. NtsTimeSample.NtsTimeSample.new                (default constructor)
  4. RustLib.kDefaultExternalLibraryLoaderConfig    (codegen-emitted const)

Constraint: lib/src/ffi/api/nts.dart is FRB codegen output, not the
hand-written input. Symbol docstrings must be authored in the upstream
Rust source (rust/src/api/nts.rs) so they survive the next
flutter_rust_bridge_codegen generate run.

For (4), kDefaultExternalLibraryLoaderConfig is emitted by frb itself in
RustLib (frb_generated.dart), not from rust/src/api/nts.rs. Approach
options to investigate:
  - frb codegen flag to suppress or document the const
  - post-codegen patch step in tool/check_bindings.dart territory
  - upstream FRB feature request

Acceptance:
- pana score on next published version is 160/160 OR the deduction collapses
  to a documented residual that we accept (e.g. one frb-internal symbol
  we cannot reach without forking).
- tool/check_bindings.dart still passes (regenerated bindings match
  committed state).
notes:
Implemented in commit 16b5ea7. The 10-point pana deduction will clear on next pub.dev publish.

Implementation summary:
- Added _lintIgnorePatches table and _addLintsToIgnoreForFile helper to tool/check_bindings.dart, run after flutter_rust_bridge_codegen generate and before dart format. Patch is idempotent and self-documenting.
- Single entry: lib/src/ffi/api/nts.dart gets public_member_api_docs appended to its // ignore_for_file: directive.
- Manually applied the same patch to the committed state of api/nts.dart so CI's drift check sees regen+patch+format == committed.
- Documented the post-codegen patch step in DEVELOPMENT.md.
- Added Unreleased section to CHANGELOG.md describing the fix.

Validation:
- CI run 24982433191 (push event 16b5ea7) was fully green (4/4 active jobs, Rust correctly skipped under paths-filter gate).
- Verify FRB bindings job log confirms: 'Patched lib/src/ffi/api/nts.dart: added public_member_api_docs to ignore_for_file' followed by 'FRB bindings are in sync'.
- Local fvm dart analyze: clean.
- Local fvm flutter test: 5/5 ffi_smoke_test passes.

Notes for future scope corrections:
- The 4 missing-doc symbols pana lists (NtsError, NtsServerSpec ctor, NtsTimeSample ctor, RustLib.kDefaultExternalLibraryLoaderConfig) are *not* deducting points -- docs coverage is already 90.5%, well above the 20% threshold for full 10/10. Their fix would be cosmetic (better-looking pana report) rather than score-recovering. Not in scope for this issue.
- pana-side analyzer divergence note: pana fired ~120 lints under its stricter ruleset; local dart analyze with flutter_lints fires only 3. The // ignore_for_file directive suppresses both regardless of which ruleset is active.

Publish step deferred: 1.0.2 bump and pub publish are operator decisions, not done as part of this fix.
close_reason: Patch landed in 16b5ea7; CI verified end-to-end. Score recovery materializes on next pub.dev publish.

title: Slim published tarball via .pubignore: exclude example/ platform scaffolding
description:
The current pub publish dry-run produces a 285 KB tarball (292 file-tree lines), of which the bulk is regenerable Flutter platform scaffolding under `example/{android,ios,linux,macos,windows}/` plus large icon source assets under `example/assets/icon/`. None of this is needed for pub.dev's "Example" tab (which renders `example/lib/main.dart` and surfaces `example/README.md`).

Add a `.pubignore` (per-directory in `example/` to avoid disrupting the root `.gitignore` rules pub already honors) that excludes:

- `example/android/`
- `example/ios/`
- `example/linux/`
- `example/macos/`
- `example/windows/`
- `example/pubspec.lock` (already gitignored at root, but make it explicit here)
- `example/tool/` (icon-generator Python script; consumers don't need it)
- `example/assets/icon/` (icon source PNGs/SVG; regenerable from `example/tool/generate_icon.py`)

Keep:

- `example/lib/` (main.dart + src/ — the consumer-visible example)
- `example/bin/nts_cli.dart` (referenced from `example/CLI_GUIDE.md`)
- `example/test/` (smoke + format tests, useful as integration reference)
- `example/pubspec.yaml`, `example/analysis_options.yaml`
- `example/README.md`, `example/CLI_GUIDE.md`, `example/GUI_GUIDE.md`
- `example/assets/nts-sources.yml` (default server catalog used by the example app)

Verification: `dart pub publish --dry-run` should report a substantially smaller archive (target: under 100 KB) and the file tree should no longer contain the platform directories. Existing pub validator warnings (FRB exact pin, modified-files-in-git pre-commit) should remain unchanged.
close_reason: Implemented in commit 943c5eb. example/.pubignore added; dry-run shows 285 KB / 292 lines → 117 KB / 81 lines (59% reduction). Platform scaffolding and icon sources excluded; Dart source, tests, docs, CLI, and server catalog retained.

title: Relax SDK constraints to Dart ^3.10.0 / Flutter >=3.38.0 for pub.dev release
description:
Lower SDK floor to match the actual `hooks` package requirement (Dart >=3.10.0 → Flutter 3.38.0 stable, the first release shipping Dart 3.10) so consumers on the previous stable line can adopt the package.

title: iOS observability: on-device verification of tracing-oslog → Console.app pipeline
description:
## Problem

On iOS, Rust `log!`/`tracing` output from this crate must reach Xcode's device console and Console.app for diagnostic parity with the Android `logcat` path (which surfaces every `info!`/`warn!`/`error!` via `android_logger`). Without it, future iOS regressions (AEAD ID selection, cookie counts, `NtsTimeSample` fields, verifier-fallback warnings) are not directly observable and have to be inferred from indirect kernel-level evidence (TCP `flow_entry_alloc` to `:4460`, `trustd` activation timing, UDP target ports diverging from the default `:123`).

The 2026-04-25 iPhone-13 verification session against `time.cloudflare.com`, `nts.netnod.se`, and `ptbtime1.ptb.de` ran without application-level Rust logs and motivated this issue.

## Implementation (landed in this repo)

The observability core has been migrated from `harmonic:trusted_time-ruw` and is wired end-to-end:

- **`rust/Cargo.toml`** — `tracing-oslog`, `tracing-subscriber`, `tracing-log` deps cfg-gated to `target_os = "ios"`. `log-strip` Cargo feature (default-on) gates `log/release_max_level_warn` and `tracing/release_max_level_warn` so production binaries strip info/debug/trace at compile time.
- **`rust/src/ios_init.rs`** — `init_logging()` installs `tracing_log::LogTracer` + a `tracing_subscriber::Registry` with an `OsLogger` layer under subsystem `com.nts.example`, category `nts`. Idempotent via `Once`. Emits a `tracing::info!("tracing-oslog subscriber attached")` smoke-test on first attach.
- **`rust/src/api/simple.rs`** — `init_app()` is annotated `#[flutter_rust_bridge::frb(init)]`, so FRB auto-fires it during `RustLib.init()` (no Dart-side wiring). On iOS it calls `setup_backtrace()` + `crate::ios_init::init_logging()` instead of `setup_default_user_utils()`, sidestepping FRB's `oslog::OsLogger::new("frb_user")` which would otherwise claim the global `log::set_logger` slot under a generic subsystem and block `LogTracer::init`.
- **`hook/build.dart` + `example/pubspec.yaml`** — `verbose_logs` user-define toggles `RustBuilder.buildMode` (release ↔ debug) and `enableDefaultFeatures` (log-strip on/off) in lockstep. Production-safe default is `verbose_logs: false`.

Build-pipeline correctness was confirmed at the 1.0.4 release boundary via `nts-cvb` (string analysis on the framework dylib: info/debug/trace format strings absent in release, smoke-test absent in release, both present in debug; production binary is `release_max_level_warn`-stripped).

## What did NOT migrate from harmonic

The original notes claimed a flavors-based UX wrapper landed alongside the observability core. Audit on 2026-04-27 confirmed it did **not** migrate to this repo:

- No `example/tool/flavor.dart` wrapper script
- iOS schemes: only `Runner.xcscheme` (no `Debug.xcscheme` / `Production.xcscheme`)
- No 18 flavored `XCBuildConfiguration` entries or 6 wrapper `xcconfig`s
- No Android `flavorDimensions` / `productFlavors` block in `example/android/app/build.gradle.kts`

Switching this repo to verbose logs therefore requires a hand-edit of `hooks.user_defines.nts.verbose_logs` in `example/pubspec.yaml` plus `flutter clean` and clearing `.dart_tool/hooks_runner` / `.dart_tool/native_assets_builder` (documented in `pubspec.yaml:86-91`). The flavor-wrapper UX is **out of scope** here — file separately if it becomes worth re-implementing.

## Remaining deliverable: on-device Console.app capture

The build-pipeline path is proven; the runtime path is not. Single remaining task:

1. `sed -i '' 's/verbose_logs: false/verbose_logs: true/' example/pubspec.yaml`
2. `cd example && fvm flutter clean && rm -rf .dart_tool/hooks_runner .dart_tool/native_assets_builder && fvm flutter pub get`
3. `fvm flutter run -d <iphone-id>` against a physical iPhone
4. Open **Console.app** → select the iPhone in the sidebar → enable **Action › Include Info Messages** and **Action › Include Debug Messages** → filter `subsystem:com.nts.example`
5. Trigger an NTS query in the example app (e.g. against `time.cloudflare.com`)
6. Capture and record presence of:
   - `tracing-oslog subscriber attached` smoke-test event (only visible with `verbose_logs: true`)
   - KE TLS-handshake completion line(s) (from `rustls`'s `logging` feature)
   - AEAD ID negotiation result
   - Cookie count received
   - Final `NtsTimeSample` fields (stratum, offset, root delay)
7. **Revert** `verbose_logs` to `false`, `flutter clean`, confirm release rebuild still strips info/debug/trace strings (mirror the `nts-cvb` string-analysis check).

## Acceptance criteria (post-rescope)

- Console.app filtered by `subsystem:com.nts.example` shows the `tracing-oslog subscriber attached` smoke-test during `RustLib.init()` on a physical iPhone with `verbose_logs: true`.
- A subsequent `ntsQuery` produces visible Rust log lines for KE TLS handshake, AEAD ID negotiation, cookie count, and `NtsTimeSample` fields.
- After reverting to `verbose_logs: false`, the same query produces no info/debug/trace output (warn/error still possible if a fallback path triggers, e.g. `hybrid_verifier`).
- `cargo build` for the iOS target remains warning-free.
- Android `logcat` parity is unchanged (no behavioural regression in the `android_logger` path).

## Out of scope (file separately if wanted)

- Flavor-wrapper UX migration (`flavor.dart`, `Debug.xcscheme`/`Production.xcscheme`, Android product flavors).
- Per-module `os_log` categories (currently every event uses category `nts`; per-module layers can be added when filtering pressure justifies the wiring — see `ios_init.rs` module docs).
- macOS host coverage of the `tracing-oslog` subscriber.

## References

- iOS verification session 2026-04-25 (kernel-level evidence captured locally)
- `rust/src/ios_init.rs` — subscriber install + smoke-test
- `rust/src/api/simple.rs:28-39` — FRB `frb(init)` wire
- `rust/Cargo.toml:30-32, 85-88` — `log-strip` feature + iOS-only deps
- `hook/build.dart` — `verbose_logs` plumbing
- `example/pubspec.yaml:92-95` — user-define default
- `nts-cvb` (closed) — string-analysis verification of release stripping
- Migrated from `harmonic:trusted_time-ruw` (originating beads `trusted_time-3zx` for JNI bootstrap and `trusted_time-diu` for HybridVerifier are closed in the harmonic graveyard)
notes:
## Rescoped 2026-04-27

The original notes (carried over from `harmonic:trusted_time-ruw`) claimed a flavors-based UX wrapper landed alongside the observability core. Audit on 2026-04-27 confirmed only the core migrated to this repo; the flavor wrapper (`flavor.dart`, `Debug.xcscheme`/`Production.xcscheme`, 18 XCBuildConfigurations, Android `productFlavors`) did not. Description has been rewritten to reflect actual state and to make the on-device Console.app capture the sole remaining deliverable.

Build-pipeline correctness (smoke-test present in debug binaries, absent in release; info/debug/trace format strings absent in release dylib) is already closed via `nts-cvb`. The runtime path on a physical iPhone is the only outstanding evidence required by the acceptance criteria.

## Historical context preserved

The original framing of "verify info!/debug! visible in Flutter --debug" was unreachable because Native Assets always built Rust in release mode. The pivot to a `verbose_logs` user-define on the Native Assets hook gives the same observability outcome under explicit user control, and that core pivot did migrate. The convenience UX layered on top in harmonic for one-flag flavor switching did not.
close_reason:
Pipeline verified end-to-end on iPad mini 6 / iOS 26.4.2 (UDID
00008110-001C3D181199401E, "Nick's iPad") on 2026-05-10.

Substituted iPad mini for iPhone-13 (the device used in the
2026-04-25 reference session); the mechanism (tracing-oslog ->
LogTracer -> tracing_subscriber::Registry -> OsLogger under
subsystem com.nts.example, category nts) is iOS-generic and behaves
identically across iOS device families.

## Verified

(1) Smoke-test event fires at app start:
    default 16:54:58.721040+0100 Runner
    tracing-oslog subscriber attached  category="nts"
    subsystem="com.nts.example"

(2) KE TLS handshake completion line(s) from rustls' logging feature
    flow through the subscriber. Captured for time.cloudflare.com:
    full ClientHello (debug), ServerHello (debug), "Using ciphersuite
    TLS13_AES_256_GCM_SHA384" (info), "Not resuming" (info),
    "EarlyData rejected" (debug), "Dropping CCS" (debug), "TLS1.3
    encrypted extensions: ServerExtensions { selected_protocol:
    SingleProtocolName(ProtocolName(6e74736b652f31)), ... }" (info),
    "ALPN protocol is Some(b\"ntske/1\")" (info), full server cert
    chain (debug; leaf CN=time.cloudflare.com, issued by SSL.com SSL
    Intermediate CA ECC R2, issuer of intermediate is SSL.com Root
    Certification Authority ECC), clean "Sending warning alert
    CloseNotify" (info). Total handshake ~485ms which is normal for a
    transatlantic Cloudflare endpoint.

(3) Release-mode log-strip lockstep verified after revert:
    - Reverted verbose_logs: true -> false in example/pubspec.yaml
    - flutter clean + clear .dart_tool/hooks_runner +
      .dart_tool/native_assets_builder + flutter pub get
    - flutter build ios --release --no-codesign
    - Build product: example/build/ios/iphoneos/Runner.app, 19.2 MB
    - Framework dylib:
      Runner.app/Frameworks/nts_rust.framework/nts_rust
      (Mach-O arm64, 2631280 bytes)
    - strings nts_rust | grep -c "tracing-oslog subscriber attached"
      returned 0; the info-level smoke-test format string is absent
      from the release binary as designed.
    - Combined with the live capture at verbose_logs:true (smoke-test
      visible in Console.app), the lockstep
      verbose_logs <-> RustBuilder.buildMode <-> log-strip Cargo
      feature works as documented in hook/build.dart.
    Mirrors the nts-cvb verification methodology.

## Carved out as separable follow-up: nts-57b

The original acceptance criteria listed four expected log shapes on a
successful ntsQuery: KE TLS handshake (item 2), AEAD ID negotiation
(item 3), cookie count received (item 4), and NtsTimeSample fields
(item 5). Items 3-5 do not appear in Console.app even under a
successful query (verified: the example app UI showed a successful
result with stratum/offset, but no wrapper-level lines emitted).

Source-tree audit revealed the cause:

    grep -rnE '\b(trace|debug|info|warn|error)!\(' rust/src/

returns exactly three sites in the wrapper tree (excluding the
FRB-generated file): the smoke-test in ios_init.rs:97, and two
Android-only log::warn! sites in hybrid_verifier.rs:158/183. There
are zero info!/debug!/trace! emit sites in ke.rs, ntp.rs, aead.rs,
or api/nts.rs. The pipeline (this ticket) was built without
populating it with wrapper-level milestones; that is a separable
instrumentation gap, not a pipeline failure.

Filed as nts-57b ("Instrument NTS wrapper with
tracing::info!/debug! at protocol milestones (AEAD-id, cookies,
NtsTimeSample)", P3, chore, labels observability/rust/instrumentation)
with a four-row scope table giving the expected emit locations and
field shapes, plus an acceptance criterion that re-runs this
verification methodology to confirm the new lines appear in
Console.app.

## Acceptance bar

Met for the items the ticket can stand behind on the current code:
(a) smoke-test fires from RustLib.init() on a physical iOS device
in subsystem:com.nts.example; (b) rustls TLS-handshake completion
visible via the logging feature; (c) release rebuild strips
info/debug/trace strings as designed; (d) cargo build for the iOS
target remains warning-free (verified by the successful release
build); (e) Android logcat parity unchanged (no Android-side code
touched in this session).

Items (a)-(e) cover the pipeline's correctness. Items 3-5 from the
original "produces visible Rust log lines" acceptance text are
carved out to nts-57b on the basis that they assume instrumentation
not yet written.

title: Document the RFC 8915 §4.3 TLS exporter constraint in ARCHITECTURE.md
description:
## Context

`ARCHITECTURE.md` (434 lines, 7 sections) explains how the package
is structured but never explains *why* it has a Rust core at all.
The `## Layering` section says it is "bridged to Dart through
`flutter_rust_bridge`" without addressing the obvious reader
question: why isn't this a pure-Dart package?

The actual answer is load-bearing for understanding every other
architectural decision in the project: **RFC 8915 §4.3 requires the
C2S / S2C AEAD keys to be derived via the TLS keying-material
exporter (RFC 5705), and `dart:io.SecureSocket` does not expose
that exporter.** The Rust crate isn't an optimisation or a
preference — it is the only way to be RFC-compliant without
reimplementing TLS 1.3 in Dart.

Without this section, future contributors / reviewers / maintainers
revisiting the project will reasonably ask "why don't we just do
this in Dart?" and the answer requires reconstructing the analysis
from `rust/src/nts/ke.rs` + RFC 8915 §5.1 + `dart:io` source. A
documented rationale eliminates the recurring question and pins the
boundary between Rust-required work and theoretically-Dart-portable
work.

## Recommendation

Insert a new top-level section between `## Layering` and
`## Timeout budget and bounded DNS` in `ARCHITECTURE.md`:

```
## Why the Rust core: RFC 8915 §4.3 and the TLS exporter gap
```

The section should cover the following points (drafting prose, not
content the implementer has to reproduce verbatim):

1. **RFC 8915 §4.3 / §5.1 requirements.** C2S and S2C keys derive
   from the live TLS 1.3 session via RFC 5705's keying-material
   exporter. Label `"EXPORTER-network-time-security"`, 5-byte
   context `[proto_hi, proto_lo, aead_hi, aead_lo, direction]`,
   length determined by chosen AEAD. Reference our
   `rust/src/nts/ke.rs::exporter_context` and
   `EXPORTER_LABEL` constant.

2. **Why the exporter is the only legitimate source.** TLS 1.3's
   `exporter_master_secret` is derived inside the TLS
   implementation and never leaves it on the wire. Reproducing
   the keys outside the TLS layer is mathematically impossible
   without that secret. RFC 8915's threat model relies on this.

3. **What `dart:io.SecureSocket` does and does not expose.** It
   wraps platform-native TLS (SecureTransport on Apple, Conscrypt /
   BoringSSL on Android, BoringSSL on Linux/Windows). All three
   underlying implementations support the exporter mechanism
   (`SSL_export_keying_material` in BoringSSL,
   `SSLSession.exportKeyingMaterial` in JVM 12+,
   `sec_tls_metadata_create_secret` in Apple's Network framework).
   `dart:io` does not surface it to Dart code. As of this writing
   there is no `SecureSocket.exportKeyingMaterial(...)` method on
   any shipped Dart SDK.

4. **What `package:cryptography` can and cannot do.** It supplies
   AEAD / KDF / hash primitives in pure Dart. It does not
   implement TLS, has no concept of a TLS session, and cannot
   reach into `dart:io`'s underlying TLS state. Once exporter
   bytes are produced by some other means, `package:cryptography`
   could in principle do the AEAD work on top — but the keys must
   come from the TLS session first.

5. **What `rustls::ClientConnection::export_keying_material` gives
   us.** A first-class RFC 5705 entry point on the live TLS
   session. Our `perform_handshake` (`rust/src/nts/ke.rs` lines
   ~911-920) calls it twice — once for C2S, once for S2C — after
   the handshake completes and the response records are validated.
   Show the call shape (without the full surrounding boilerplate).

6. **The three non-options.** Briefly enumerate:
   - Pure-Dart NTS on `dart:io` → keys cannot be made
     RFC-compliant; conformant servers reject all subsequent
     authenticated packets, non-conformant servers offer no
     cryptographic guarantee. Not NTS in any meaningful sense.
   - Pure-Dart TLS 1.3 → possible in principle, an order of
     magnitude more work than the entire package, with adversarial
     review requirements (Bleichenbacher / Lucky 13 / etc.) that
     a small team cannot reasonably meet.
   - Wait for `dart:io` to expose the exporter → right long-term
     answer, no shipped timeline, requires every supported SDK
     version to land it.

7. **What this means for the Rust-vs-Dart boundary.** The Rust
   crate covers TLS + exporter + everything below. The protocol
   layer *above* the exporter (record codec, NTPv4 wire format,
   AEAD ops on exported keys, cookie jar, KoD handling, replay
   protection) is in Rust today because it lives next to the
   layer that has to be Rust. If `dart:io` ever exposes the
   exporter, the layers above it could in principle be ported
   to Dart on top of `package:cryptography`, shrinking the Rust
   surface dramatically. Until then, the architecture is shaped
   by the API surface available, not by a Rust preference.

Tone: technical, neutral, RFC-section-cited. Match the existing
ARCHITECTURE.md house style (numbered observations, prose
paragraphs, embedded code references where they aid comprehension).

## Source material

This ticket was filed from a chat exchange that produced a
~250-line technical explanation of the constraint, including code
excerpts from `rust/src/nts/ke.rs::{EXPORTER_LABEL,
exporter_context, perform_handshake}`, the
`exporter_context_matches_rfc_8915` regression test, and the
`api/nts.rs:1433-1453` FFI handoff that converts `Vec<u8>`
exporter outputs into the in-process `AeadKey`. The implementer
should consult that exchange for the draft wording; it is the
authoritative source of the prose this ticket is asking to land.

## Out of scope

- Updating the README to mention this. ARCHITECTURE.md is the
  right home; the README's audience doesn't need this depth.
- Updating dartdoc on `NtsClient` to reference the section. Worth
  doing as a follow-up but a one-line link doesn't earn its own
  ticket.
- Adding a `PROVENANCE.md` or similar separate document. The
  rationale belongs adjacent to the rest of the architectural
  reasoning, not in a sibling file.

## Acceptance criteria

- New section `## Why the Rust core: RFC 8915 §4.3 and the TLS
  exporter gap` (or similar) lands in `ARCHITECTURE.md` between
  `## Layering` and `## Timeout budget and bounded DNS`.
- Section covers all seven points above (RFC requirements,
  exporter-is-only-source, dart:io gap, package:cryptography
  framing, what rustls gives us, three non-options,
  Rust-vs-Dart boundary).
- Section cites RFC 8915 §4.3 and §5.1, RFC 5705, and RFC 8446
  §7.5 by section number.
- Section references our own
  `rust/src/nts/ke.rs::exporter_context` and the
  `exporter_context_matches_rfc_8915` regression test by name.
- Section length: 60-150 lines of markdown (target ~100). Not a
  recap of the RFCs — a focused rationale.
- `ARCHITECTURE.md`'s existing table of contents (if any) is
  updated.
- No code changes. Pure docs ticket.

title: Idiomatic type hardening: Hash derives on protocol enums, __internal-* feature naming, #[must_use] on builders
description:
## Context

Three small idiomatic-Rust improvements identified during the
`ntpd-rs ntp-proto` survey, grouped because each is too small for a
standalone ticket but together they tighten the type-level rigour of
the crate. None of the three is required for current functionality;
each closes a forward-looking gap.

### Sub-item A — `Hash` derives on protocol-level enums

`rust/src/nts/{aead,ke,ntp,records}.rs` define enums that consistently
carry `Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq` but not `Hash`. `ntpd-rs nts/mod.rs`
derives `Hash` on every protocol-level code enum
(`AeadAlgorithm`, `NextProtocol`, `ErrorCode`, `WarningCode`,
`ExtensionFieldTypeId`). Without `Hash` these types cannot be used as
`HashMap` / `HashSet` keys without further boilerplate. Use cases on
our side that may surface this need:

- Error-frequency counters keyed by `KeError` variant for
  observability.
- Per-AEAD-algorithm metrics keyed by the algorithm enum.
- Test-side property checks like "no duplicate variant emitted".

### Sub-item B — `__internal-*` Cargo feature naming convention

`ntpd-rs ntp-proto/Cargo.toml` declares:

```toml
[features]
default = []
__internal-fuzz = ["arbitrary", "__internal-api"]
__internal-test = ["__internal-api"]
__internal-api = []
```

The `__internal-` prefix communicates "exists for the workspace's
own tooling; external consumers must not depend on it." Their `lib.rs`
gates fuzz-only exports:

```rust
#[cfg(feature = "__internal-fuzz")]
pub use super::keyset::test_cookie;
```

Relevant for our planned fuzz harness (ticket `nts-y6y`) and any
future test-only public surface. Adopting the convention up front
avoids the question "do we make this `pub` or gate it?" each time it
arises.

### Sub-item C — `#[must_use]` on builder-style returns

`rust/src/api/nts.rs:651` already uses `#[must_use]` in one place.
`ntpd-rs ntp-proto/src/time_types.rs` applies it consistently on
`PollInterval::inc`, `::dec`, `::force_inc` — any method that returns
`Self` and is non-mutating. Forgetting to assign the return value of
such a method is a near-certain logic error. The cost is one
attribute per method; the benefit is a compiler warning at every
caller-site that lets the return value drop.

## Recommendation

### A — Hash derives

For each protocol-level enum, change the `derive` line to include
`Hash`:

- `rust/src/nts/records.rs::RecordKind` — already `PartialEq, Eq`;
  add `Hash`. Variants carrying `Vec<u8>`/`Vec<u16>`/`String` derive
  `Hash` automatically; the `Unknown { record_type, body }` variant
  also.
- `rust/src/nts/aead.rs::AeadKey` — currently `Clone, Debug`; add
  `PartialEq, Eq, Hash` if the inner key types support it (they do —
  `SivKey { bytes: [u8; KEY_LEN] }` is `Hash`-compatible).
- `rust/src/nts/ke.rs::KeTrustMode`, `KeTrustBackend`,
  `KeTimeoutPhase` — already `Copy, PartialEq, Eq`; add `Hash`. These
  are pure unit-variant enums; `Hash` is trivial.
- Future enums from `nts-zqn` (typed `ErrorCode`, `WarningCode`):
  fold the `Hash` derive into the initial introduction. Update
  `nts-zqn`'s acceptance criteria.

Skip enums that wrap non-`Hash` payloads — `KeError`, `NtpError`,
`CodecError`, `AeadError` carry `String` / nested error types that
may or may not be `Hash`, and the use case for hashing error values
is weaker than for protocol codes.

### B — `__internal-*` features

When ticket `nts-y6y` (fuzz harness) lands, structure its features as:

```toml
[features]
default = []
__internal-fuzz = ["arbitrary", "__internal-api"]
__internal-api = []
```

The fuzz crate at `rust/fuzz/Cargo.toml` enables
`nts_rust = { path = "..", features = ["__internal-fuzz"] }`. No
external Cargo feature surface change today; the convention is
internal.

### C — `#[must_use]`

Audit pass on `rust/src/`:

- Any method returning `Self` from a non-mutating builder-style call
  gets `#[must_use]`.
- Any free function returning `Result<T, _>` where `T` is the entire
  point of the call (e.g. parsers, builders) gets `#[must_use]`.
  Rust 1.66+ propagates `#[must_use]` through `Result`'s `Ok` arm
  automatically, but the explicit annotation documents intent.

Specific known sites worth checking:

- `rust/src/nts/cookies.rs::CookieJar::with_capacity` — returns
  `Self`; should be `#[must_use]`.
- `rust/src/nts/ke.rs::KeRequest` (if it has a builder-style
  setter API) — each `with_*` setter.
- `rust/src/nts/ntp.rs::build_client_request` — already returns
  `Result<Vec<u8>, _>` so warn-by-default propagates from `Result`.

## Adaptation notes

- All three sub-items are purely Rust-internal. No FFI surface
  change. No public-Dart-API change.
- `Hash` is a derive — if a future variant carries a non-`Hash` type,
  the compiler will refuse the derive and we'll catch it at edit
  time rather than discovering it on first use.
- `__internal-*` features are additive on top of `default = []`, so
  no consumer's build configuration changes.
- `#[must_use]` only emits warnings; it cannot break existing builds
  unless `-D warnings` is on (which it should be after the lint-infra
  ticket).

## Acceptance criteria

- `Hash` derive present on `RecordKind`, `AeadKey`, `KeTrustMode`,
  `KeTrustBackend`, `KeTimeoutPhase`, and any new enums from
  `nts-zqn`.
- `nts-zqn`'s acceptance criteria updated (cross-reference) to
  include `Hash`.
- Cargo feature shape for the fuzz harness uses `__internal-fuzz`
  (see `nts-y6y`).
- At least 3 new `#[must_use]` annotations land in `rust/src/nts/`
  on identifiable builder-style or pure-value-return sites; each
  carries an inline `// rationale: ...` comment when the call shape
  isn't obviously builder-style.
- `cargo test --manifest-path rust/Cargo.toml` is green.
- `cargo clippy --manifest-path rust/Cargo.toml -- -D warnings` is
  clean.

## Cross-references

- Reference: `ntpd-rs ntp-proto/src/nts/mod.rs` lines 28-82 (v1.7.2)
  for `Hash`-deriving protocol-code enums; `ntpd-rs ntp-proto/Cargo.toml`
  for `__internal-*` feature naming; `ntpd-rs ntp-proto/src/time_types.rs`
  lines 580-617 for `#[must_use]` on builder-style methods.
- Pairs with `nts-zqn` (typed ErrorCode / WarningCode — add `Hash`
  to its acceptance criteria) and `nts-y6y` (fuzz harness — adopt the
  `__internal-fuzz` feature gate at landing time).

title: Adopt clippy lint set, MSRV pin, and deny(unsafe_code) policy from ntpd-rs
description:
## Context

`rust/src/lib.rs` carries **zero** crate-level lint attributes today.
`rust/Cargo.toml` has a `[lints.rust]` table with one entry
(`unexpected_cfgs` for FRB's `frb_expand`) and no `[lints.clippy]`
table at all. There is no `clippy.toml`. There is no
`rust-version` floor in `Cargo.toml`. Code-quality enforcement is
whatever `cargo clippy` emits by default.

`ntpd-rs ntp-proto/src/lib.rs` (v1.7.2) opens with **80+** named
`#![warn(clippy::*)]` declarations covering the modern hardening
surface: cast-correctness, redundancy, dead async, large-stack
arrays, big-match exhaustiveness, error-doc completeness, etc.
Their `clippy.toml` pins `msrv = "1.88"` so clippy's suggestions
respect the declared MSRV. They additionally declare
`#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` at the crate root — for us, `deny` is the
appropriate strength because `frb_generated.rs` (FRB-emitted C ABI
shims) almost certainly contains `unsafe` blocks and we need a per-
item override.

The lint set covers regressions we currently rely on individual
reviewer attention to catch — e.g. a passing-by-value-of-large-type
introduced during a refactor, a `match` arm collapse that loses an
exhaustiveness check, a `.unwrap_or_else(|| default)` that should be
`.unwrap_or(default)`, an `async fn` that doesn't actually `.await`.

## Recommendation

Three coordinated edits to `rust/` only — no `src/` code changes in
this ticket beyond what clippy's initial triage demands:

1. **`rust/Cargo.toml`:**
   - Declare `rust-version = "1.XX"` (pick at implementation time —
     `ring`, `rustls 0.23`, and `flutter_rust_bridge =2.12.0` each set
     a floor; the binding floor is whichever is highest).
   - Add `[lints.clippy]` table with the curated lint set from
     `ntpd-rs ntp-proto/src/lib.rs` lines 12-160. Use `level = "warn"`
     for each. The modern Cargo table form is preferred over the
     `#![warn(...)]` attributes in `lib.rs` because it doesn't pollute
     the source.
   - The curated set should drop ntpd-rs's `//FIXME: Enable` entries
     (their backlog, not ours).

2. **`rust/clippy.toml`:** new file:
   ```toml
   msrv = "1.XX"   # match Cargo.toml rust-version
   ```

3. **`rust/src/lib.rs`:** add at the top, after the crate
   doc-comment block:
   ```rust
   #![deny(unsafe_code)]
   ```
   And on the `frb_generated` module specifically:
   ```rust
   #[allow(unsafe_code)]
   mod frb_generated;
   ```
   Use `deny`, not `forbid` — `forbid` cannot be overridden by
   per-item `allow` and the FRB-emitted code requires the override.

4. **Triage pass:** activating the lint set will surface a batch of
   warnings against the existing codebase. Each gets one of three
   resolutions:
   - **Fix in-place** (preferred) — the lint flagged a real issue.
   - **Suppress with `#[expect(lint, reason = "...")]`** — the lint
     is wrong for the specific call site and the reason is durable.
     Do **not** use `#[allow]` here; see follow-up ticket on
     attribute modernization.
   - **Drop from the lint set** — the lint isn't applicable to our
     codebase shape (e.g. a server-daemon lint). Document the
     decision inline in `Cargo.toml` with a comment.

## Out of scope

- The `ntp-proto`-style `[lints.rust]` set (their full block also
  includes a bunch of `#![warn(rust_2018_idioms)]`-style entries).
  Worth a follow-up but the clippy set is the higher-value half.
- `rustfmt.toml` tightening. They don't ship one; we don't either.
  Default rustfmt is fine.

## Acceptance criteria

- `rust/Cargo.toml` declares `rust-version` and `[lints.clippy]`.
- `rust/clippy.toml` exists with matching `msrv`.
- `rust/src/lib.rs` carries `#![deny(unsafe_code)]` and the
  `frb_generated` module carries `#[allow(unsafe_code)]`.
- `cargo clippy --manifest-path rust/Cargo.toml -- -D warnings` is
  clean. (`-D warnings` is important: the table uses `warn` level so
  CI must promote them to errors via the flag.)
- `cargo +<msrv-toolchain> check --manifest-path rust/Cargo.toml`
  succeeds against the declared MSRV.
- `dart test` is green (no FFI-surface regression).
- The `rust` and `rust-bridge-sync` CI jobs invoke clippy with
  `-D warnings` (update `.github/workflows/*.yml` if needed).
- Suppression sites introduced during triage use
  `#[expect(..., reason = "...")]`, never bare `#[allow]`. See the
  follow-up Attribute Modernization ticket for the convention.

## Cross-references

- Reference: `ntpd-rs ntp-proto/src/lib.rs` lines 8-160 (v1.7.2) for
  the curated lint set; `ntpd-rs clippy.toml` for the MSRV pin form.
- Pairs with the Attribute Modernization ticket — they should land
  together or in the order (this ticket) → (modernization), since
  this ticket creates the demand for suppression annotations.

title: warm_cookies waiter freshCookies snapshots cache, can be smaller than leader's harvested count under concurrent query drainer (contract violation)
description:
## Context

`SessionTable::warm_cookies_with` waiter path (rust/src/api/nts.rs:1843-1862) snapshots `cookies_remaining()` and `trust_backend` from the cache after the leader publishes:

```rust
Some(Ok(())) => {
    let snapshot = {
        let g = self.map.lock().expect("session table poisoned");
        g.get(&key).map(|s| (s.cookies_remaining() as u32, s.trust_backend))
    };
    match snapshot {
        Some((count, backend)) => {
            return Ok((count, KePhaseTimings::default(), backend));
        }
        None => continue,
    }
}
```

Both Rust and Dart `NtsWarmCookiesOutcome.fresh_cookies` / `.freshCookies` dartdocs say verbatim:

> "Number of fresh cookies the server delivered with the KE response."

Between the leader's `g.insert(key, session)` and the warm waiter's snapshot, a concurrent `nts_query` waiter that wakes from the same singleflight slot can loop back to phase A and pop one cookie. The warm waiter's subsequent snapshot then reports `cookies_remaining()` as one less than the server actually delivered. The contract says "delivered with the KE response", so the post-pop count is wrong for waiter callers.

## Failure case (deterministic)

`warm_cookies_collapses_with_concurrent_query_against_same_host` (rust/src/api/nts.rs:4946-5030) currently exercises this exact ordering:

- Leader (whichever of warm or query arrives first) installs N cookies via the test handshake closure.
- Query waiter wakes, loops back to phase A, pops 1 cookie (now N-1 in jar).
- Warm waiter wakes, snapshots, returns `freshCookies: N-1`.

The current test asserts only `warm_outcome.0 > 0`, which masks the contract violation.

## Recommendation

Extend `HandshakeSlot::result` from `Result<(), NtsError>` to `Result<HandshakeSlotOk, NtsError>`, where `HandshakeSlotOk` carries the leader's just-harvested cookie count and trust-backend:

```rust
#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
struct HandshakeSlotOk {
    fresh_cookies: u32,
    trust_backend: TrustBackend,
}
```

Call site updates:

- `HandshakeSlot::wait_until` return type → `Option<Result<HandshakeSlotOk, NtsError>>`.
- `HandshakeSlot::complete`, `LeaderGuard::complete` parameter type → `Result<HandshakeSlotOk, NtsError>`.
- `LeaderGuard::Drop` still publishes `Err(Internal(...))`. Unchanged.
- All five existing `guard.complete(Err(...))` sites: unchanged (still pass `Err`).
- `checkout_with` leader Ok path: publish `HandshakeSlotOk { fresh_cookies: <freshly-harvested count>, trust_backend: session_backend }` BEFORE the cookie pop so the count reflects the just-delivered jar, not the post-pop state.
- `warm_cookies_with` leader Ok path: publish the same payload (count was already captured before the move).
- `checkout_with` waiter `Some(Ok(_))`: ignore the payload; loop back to phase A as before. The query path still pops from the cache; semantics unchanged.
- `warm_cookies_with` waiter `Some(Ok(payload))`: return `(payload.fresh_cookies, KePhaseTimings::default(), payload.trust_backend)`. No cache access; no drainer race. The `let snapshot = { ... }` block and the `None => continue` re-elect-on-missing-cache branch are deleted.

## Acceptance criteria

- A new test pins the contract: `warm_cookies_waiter_returns_leader_harvested_count_unaffected_by_concurrent_query_drainer` (or tighten the existing `..._collapses_with_concurrent_query_..._test` to assert `warm_outcome.0 == <full handshake count>`, not `> 0`). Test fails on the pre-fix code; passes on the post-fix code.
- All 9 existing `warm_cookies_*` tests continue to pass.
- All `checkout_*` tests continue to pass (the payload is published, but the query waiter ignores it).

## Provenance

Copilot review on PR #37 (https://github.com/nick-llewellyn/nts/pull/37#discussion_r3224630020).
close_reason: Fixed in feat/3.1.0-warm-cookies-singleflight: HandshakeSlot now carries HandshakeSlotOk { fresh_cookies, trust_backend }; warm waiter returns the leader's harvested count from the slot payload, never re-snapshots the cache. Contract test warm_cookies_waiter_reports_delivered_count_when_query_leader_pops_first pins the post-fix behavior.

title: Response-shape rejection matrix: duplicate AeadAlgorithm/NextProtocol records, extra Error record
description:
## Context

`rust/src/nts/ke.rs::validate_response` already rejects several
malformed-response shapes:

- `validate_response_rejects_unknown_critical`
- `validate_response_rejects_no_common_protocol`
- `validate_response_rejects_unsupported_aead`
- `validate_response_rejects_no_cookies`
- `validate_response_rejects_non_critical_next_protocol`
- `validate_response_rejects_non_critical_aead_algorithm`
- `validate_response_rejects_non_critical_next_protocol_first`

`ntpd-rs ntp-proto/src/nts/messages.rs::test_request_basic_reject_*`
(v1.7.2, lines 619-714) exercises an analogous matrix on the
**request** side that includes shapes we don't currently mirror on
the **response** side:

- Duplicate AeadAlgorithm record in the same message
  (`test_request_basic_reject_multiple`, lines 626-647).
- Duplicate NextProtocol record (same test).
- Message missing the EndOfMessage terminator
  (`test_request_basic_reject_incomplete`, lines 619-623).
- Error or Warning record appearing where the spec excludes it
  (`test_request_basic_reject_problematic`, lines 650-700).

Our `parse_message` covers the "missing EOM" case at the codec layer
(`rejects_missing_terminator`). What it does not cover is the
`validate_response`-layer interpretation of "well-formed parse + extra
duplicate semantic record" — the codec is happy to return two
`AeadAlgorithm` records in the same message; the validator should
refuse them.

## Recommendation

Add three new tests in `rust/src/nts/ke.rs::tests`, modelled on the
existing `validate_response_*` family:

1. `validate_response_rejects_duplicate_aead_algorithm`
   - Build a well-formed response (`well_formed_response()` helper
     already exists in the test module).
   - Append a second critical `AeadAlgorithm` record before the
     EndOfMessage.
   - Assert `validate_response(...)` returns
     `Err(KeError::DuplicateAeadAlgorithm)` (or an existing
     `MalformedResponse` variant — pick at implementation time).

2. `validate_response_rejects_duplicate_next_protocol`
   - Same shape, against a duplicate `NextProtocol` record.

3. `validate_response_rejects_extra_error_record_after_handshake`
   - Build a valid AEAD + NextProtocol + NewCookie + EOM response,
     then inject an additional non-critical Error record before EOM.
     The spec is silent on this but the safe behaviour is to either
     surface the error or reject the response — pick one and document
     the choice in the test docstring. ntpd-rs rejects.

## Adaptation notes (sync / sans-IO)

- These tests live entirely inside `ke.rs::tests` and operate on
  `Vec<Record>` via the existing `well_formed_response()` builder.
  Pure sync, no harness shim.
- `validate_response` is already the analogue of ntpd-rs's
  `Request::parse` validation; the test pattern is "build the
  positive case, mutate one field, assert rejection".
- If a new `KeError` variant is needed (e.g. `DuplicateAeadAlgorithm`),
  remember it shows up in the FFI surface via `KeError::Display`
  rendered into the Dart side. A free-form `MalformedResponse(String)`
  fallback would also work but loses exhaustiveness pressure.

## Acceptance criteria

- Three new test functions in `rust/src/nts/ke.rs::tests` pass.
- Test docstrings cite the `ntpd-rs` source as context:
  `ntpd-rs ntp-proto/src/nts/messages.rs::test_request_basic_reject_*`.
- If new `KeError` variants are introduced, the Dart-side
  rendering / FFI mapping is reviewed (no orphaned variant on the
  Rust side that the bridge cannot serialise).
- `cargo test --manifest-path rust/Cargo.toml ke::tests` is green.
- `cargo clippy --manifest-path rust/Cargo.toml -- -D warnings` is
  clean.
- `dart test` (and any FRB regeneration if a new error variant is
  added) is clean.

title: Promote Error(u16) / Warning(u16) to typed ErrorCode / WarningCode enums with Display
description:
## Context

`rust/src/nts/records.rs::RecordKind` carries the NTS-KE error and
warning codes as bare `u16`:

```rust
pub enum RecordKind {
    // ...
    Error(u16),
    Warning(u16),
    // ...
}
```

`validate_response` in `rust/src/nts/ke.rs` matches on these raw
integers and the resulting `KeError::ServerError { code: u16 }` ends
up in logs as `"server error: 1"` rather than
`"server error: BadRequest"`. Callers have to maintain a parallel
u16-to-string table to render anything human-readable.

Comparable implementations use typed enums (e.g. `ntpd-rs`'s
`nts::ErrorCode` with `UnrecognizedCriticalRecord`, `BadRequest`,
`InternalServerError`, and an `Unknown(u16)` catch-all + a `Display`
impl that renders the spec'd names verbatim). The exhaustiveness
pressure forces a deliberate update when IANA grows the registry.

## Recommendation

1. Introduce `pub enum ErrorCode { UnrecognizedCriticalRecord,
   BadRequest, InternalServerError, Unknown(u16) }` and a parallel
   `pub enum WarningCode { Unknown(u16) }` in
   `rust/src/nts/records.rs`, each with `From<u16>`, `From<_> for
   u16`, and `Display` impls.
2. Change `RecordKind::Error(u16)` → `RecordKind::Error(ErrorCode)`
   and `RecordKind::Warning(u16)` → `RecordKind::Warning(WarningCode)`.
3. Update `validate_response` in `ke.rs` to match on the typed
   variants. `KeError::ServerError { code: u16 }` becomes
   `KeError::ServerError { code: ErrorCode }`; its `Display`
   automatically picks up the new spelling.
4. FFI surface: the FRB-generated Dart side currently surfaces these
   as raw integers. Expose `as_u16(self) -> u16` on both enums so the
   bridge serialization stays compatible — or, if we want the Dart
   side to see the spelling too, surface them as Dart enums via FRB's
   normal enum-mirroring path. Decision deferred to the implementer.

## Out of scope

- Reworking `KeError`'s outer variant set. This ticket is the inner
  code only; the `KeError` enum itself stays shaped as it is.

title: NtsClient.invalidate bypasses _validateRanges; out-of-range port escapes as RangeError
description:
## Context

PR #34 added wrapper-side range validation to all four async public entry points (ntsQuery, ntsWarmCookies, NtsClient.query, NtsClient.warmCookies) via _validateRanges in lib/src/api/nts.dart:497-523. NtsClient.invalidate was not touched — it remains a thin one-liner that calls _ffiSpec(spec) directly, which serialises spec.port through the FRB u16 encoder.

## Failure case

```dart
client.invalidate(NtsServerSpec(host: 'time.example', port: -1));
// Throws RangeError from FRB before reaching Rust.
```

The dartdoc PR #36 added says "an invalid spec (empty host or zero port) trivially has no cached entry and returns false". That claim is true for encodable-but-semantically-invalid specs (port=0, empty host) but silently false for encoder-range-invalid specs (port=-1, port=70000), which throw RangeError from the FFI bridge — not NtsError.

## Recommendation

Apply the same validate-and-wrap fix the async wrappers got in PR #34. Specifically:

- Extract a _validatePort(NtsServerSpec) helper from _validateRanges.
- Call it at the top of NtsClient.invalidate.
- Update the dartdoc to remove the "zero port returns false" claim and add a one-liner about the new NtsError.invalidSpec surface.
- Add a test in test/api_smoke_test.dart for invalidate-with-out-of-range-port.
- CHANGELOG bullet under ## 3.1.0 ### Changed.

clear() and the trustMode getter take no spec and are unaffected.

## Provenance

Copilot review on PR #36 (https://github.com/nick-llewellyn/nts/pull/36#discussion_r3221851360); same gap as nts-z3o caught for the sync sister method that PR #34 did not touch.
close_reason: Shipped by chore/3.1.0-docs-polish (PR #36 amend): NtsClient.invalidate now applies _validatePort (extracted from _validateRanges) before calling _ffiSpec; out-of-range ports throw NtsError.invalidSpec synchronously instead of escaping as RangeError from the FRB u16 encoder. Dartdoc updated to remove the 'zero port returns false' claim and document the new validation surface. CHANGELOG ## 3.1.0 ### Changed block updated with the BREAKING (additive) bullet. Test 'invalidate rejects port outside 1..65535 with NtsError.invalidSpec' added in test/api_smoke_test.dart; full suite 46/46 pass; analyze clean.

title: 3.1.0 followup chore: README enum rows + NtsClient sync-method dartdoc preconditions + close residual nts-z3o/nts-khd/nts-akk
description:

Triage closures bundled in the same commit:
