title: test(dns): exercise slow/blackholed resolver against bounded DNS path
description:
`rust/src/nts/dns.rs::resolve_with_timeout` bounds `to_socket_addrs` on a worker thread + `mpsc::recv_timeout`. The unit tests cover the fast-path (numeric loopback) and the zero-budget path (any error kind), but neither stalls a real resolver: `zero_budget_surfaces_as_timed_out` only asserts that an error returns, not that `ErrorKind::TimedOut` was the kind, because we cannot reliably block the system resolver from a `cargo test` process.

Add a deterministic test that:
1. Spawns a fake nameserver (UDP socket bound to a loopback port) that accepts queries but never responds.
2. Points the test at `localhost` via a `resolv.conf`-style override OR uses a custom `DnsResolver` injection seam carved into `resolve_with_timeout` (preferred — see follow-up below).
3. Asserts `resolve_with_timeout(..., Duration::from_millis(50))` returns `Err(io::Error)` with `kind() == ErrorKind::TimedOut` within ~100 ms.

This requires either:
- A new injection seam in `resolve_with_timeout` accepting `impl Fn(&str, u16) -> io::Result<Vec<SocketAddr>>` so the test can swap in a controlled lookup function. The production call site keeps the current `(host, port).to_socket_addrs()` closure; tests inject a `thread::sleep(...)` stub.
- OR a new feature-gated `dns_inject` module exposing the seam only under `#[cfg(test)]`.

Acceptance:
- New test passes deterministically under `cargo test --manifest-path rust/Cargo.toml` (no network dependency).
- `connect_with_timeout` and `bind_connected_udp` both gain regression tests that exercise the slow-DNS branch via the same seam.
- Existing 97 tests still pass; clippy clean under `-D warnings`.

Out of scope for the original P2 fix because:
- The synchronous architecture has no portable way to cancel an in-flight `getaddrinfo`, so the production code is correct as-is — this work is purely about **proving** the budget is honoured under adversarial DNS.
- The injection seam is a small refactor that touches the public function signature and warrants its own review.

Discovered while implementing P2 (bounded DNS resolution).
close_reason:
Implemented resolver-injection seam (`resolve_with<F>` in `nts/dns.rs`, `connect_with_timeout_using<F>` in `nts/ke.rs`, `bind_connected_udp_using<F>` in `api/nts.rs`) and added three deterministic slow-DNS regression tests covering the full call chain:

1. `nts::dns::tests::slow_resolver_surfaces_as_timed_out` — proves `resolve_with` returns `ErrorKind::TimedOut` within 5× budget when the lookup closure sleeps past the deadline.
2. `nts::ke::tests::connect_with_timeout_surfaces_slow_dns_as_timed_out` — same shape, asserts the wrapping `KeError::Io(_)` carries `ErrorKind::TimedOut` so `From<KeError> for NtsError` produces `NtsError::Timeout`.
3. `api::nts::tests::bind_connected_udp_surfaces_slow_dns_as_timeout` — top-of-stack assertion that the user-visible error is exactly `NtsError::Timeout` (not `NtsError::Network`).

Production callers go through thin `connect_with_timeout` / `bind_connected_udp` / `resolve_with_timeout` wrappers that forward `system_lookup`. No public API change; FRB-generated bindings only pick up the new private function name in their "ignored" list comment. Tests are network-free and complete in <100 ms each.

Quality gates: cargo test 100/0/3 (was 97), cargo clippy --tests --all-targets -- -D warnings clean, my new code rustfmt-clean (the pre-existing drift in 7 unrelated files remains tracked by nts-755), dart analyze clean, flutter test test/ffi_smoke_test.dart 5/5.

title: fix(example/log_view): trim trailing whitespace below newest log entry
description:
## Problem

After the auto-scroll fix in nts-v8b made stickiness reliable, it became visually obvious that the gap between the most recent log entry and the bottom edge of the log card is too large. Two compounding sources:

1. `LogView._spansFor` appended `\n` to every entry's last span — including the final one — leaving a phantom blank line at the end of `SelectableText.rich`. At `bodySmall` (~12sp) × `height: 1.35`, that's roughly 16 px of dead space below the last visible line.
2. `SingleChildScrollView` used symmetric `EdgeInsets.all(12)`. The 12 px bottom inset stacked on top of the phantom blank line for ~28 px of total trailing gutter.

## Fix

`example/lib/src/widgets/log_view.dart`:

- Removed trailing `\n` from the message span in `_spansFor`. Inserted a leading `TextSpan(text: '\n')` separator between entries at the build site so adjacent entries still render on separate lines but the last entry has no phantom blank line below it.
- Tightened `SingleChildScrollView` padding from `EdgeInsets.all(12)` to `EdgeInsets.fromLTRB(12, 12, 12, 8)`.
- Updated `_spansFor` dartdoc to document the no-trailing-newline contract.

Selection / copy via `SelectableText.rich` walks the full inline tree, so the separator spans are still part of the copied payload — pasting still yields one entry per line.

The 32 px stickiness threshold is unaffected: it tolerates scroll position vs `maxScrollExtent`, not layout spacing.

## Verification

- `flutter analyze` (example/): No issues found.
- `flutter test` (example/): 31/31 pass.
- Manual visual inspection still pending (next session) — not regression-blocking because tests assert on `NtsLogEntry.message` content, not rendered span structure.
close_reason: Implemented in same session: dropped trailing \n from _spansFor (inserted between-entries separator at build site), tightened SingleChildScrollView padding from EdgeInsets.all(12) to EdgeInsets.fromLTRB(12, 12, 12, 8). flutter analyze clean, flutter test 31/31 pass.

title: docs(rust): cookies.rs comment claims "initial NTS-KE response always delivers exactly 8" — server-policy-dependent per RFC 8915 §4
description:
## Problem

`rust/src/nts/cookies.rs:11-12` documents `DEFAULT_CAPACITY: usize = 8` with:

> /// Default per-host capacity. RFC 8915 §6 recommends 8 unused cookies and the
> /// initial NTS-KE response always delivers exactly 8.

The first half is correct (RFC 8915 §6 advises ≤ 8 unused cookies per host to bound exposure if the KE state is later compromised). The second half is **not** mandated by the protocol — RFC 8915 §4 leaves the cookie count delivered in the KE response to server policy. Cloudflare reportedly delivers 8 from `time.cloudflare.com:4460`, but NetNod and other deployments may differ.

This contradicts the example/README refactor in `nts-0ni`, which now states the count is server-policy dependent.

## Suggested fix

Replace the second sentence with something like:

> /// Default per-host capacity. RFC 8915 §6 advises clients keep at most 8
> /// unused cookies per server to bound exposure if KE state is later
> /// compromised; this matches the cap several public deployments (e.g.
> /// Cloudflare) deliver in the initial KE response.

No code change — pure doc comment. Out of scope for `nts-0ni`, which is consumer-side only.

## Non-goals

- No change to `DEFAULT_CAPACITY` value (8 stays — RFC §6 cap, well-tested).
- No FRB regen needed (private constant, not part of the FFI surface).
close_reason: Fixed in rust/src/nts/cookies.rs: replaced the inaccurate "initial NTS-KE response always delivers exactly 8" claim with wording that cites RFC 8915 §6 (client-side cap of 8) and §4 (server policy controls the count actually delivered), matching the suggested fix in the ticket. cargo fmt --check and cargo clippy --tests --all-targets -- -D warnings both clean.

title: chore(release): convert screenshots/gui_showcase.png to lossless WebP for tarball slim-down
description:
The 1.1.0 tarball is 835 KB compressed, of which `screenshots/gui_showcase.png` accounts for 801 KB (~96%). The image is a 1766×2062 RGBA PNG of the Flutter GUI showcase, registered under the `screenshots:` field in `pubspec.yaml` since 1.0.1.

Converting to lossless WebP at the native resolution drops the file to 183 KB — a 78% reduction — with zero perceptual or pixel-level loss. This brings the published tarball from 835 KB down to ~213 KB.

Defer to the next patch release (1.1.1) since 1.1.0 is already tagged, pushed, and has a published GitHub Release; slipping a non-functional polish change in now would muddy the changelog narrative.
design:
**Why lossless WebP at native resolution**

cwebp benchmarks across resolutions and codecs:

| Height | Lossless WebP | Lossy q90 | Lossy q85 |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 2062px (native) | **183 KB** | 209 KB | 185 KB |
| 1600px | 294 KB | 166 KB | 147 KB |
| 1280px | 201 KB | 117 KB | 102 KB |
| 1024px | 132 KB | 82 KB | 70 KB |
| 800px | 90 KB | 56 KB | 49 KB |

Counterintuitively, lossless WebP gets *larger* when resizing down to 1280–1600px because the source PNG is rasterized pixel-aligned by Flutter — every glyph edge sits on integer boundaries and palette compression catches the whole image (243 colors, PREDICTION + PALETTE transforms only). Resizing introduces fractional-pixel alpha-blended edges that explode the palette and force cwebp into the less efficient CROSS-COLOR-TRANSFORM + SUBTRACT-GREEN path. Lossy doesn't have this problem because it throws away high-frequency resampling artefacts anyway.

Native 2062px is also the right display target: pub.dev's lightbox caps around 1280px wide on a 1x display, so a portrait image fits to viewport height (~700–1000px on most laptops). 2x retina = 1400–2000px tall, so 2062px is essentially "2x retina lightbox" already.

**Why pub.dev prefers WebP**

Pana validates by running `webpinfo` first; non-WebP files get passed through `cwebp` server-side (see dart-lang/pub-dev#6669). Shipping `.webp` skips that conversion step. The Dart blog announcement uses `.webp` paths in its example.

**Implementation**

```bash
cwebp -lossless -z 9 -m 6 \
  screenshots/gui_showcase.png \
  -o screenshots/gui_showcase.webp
git rm screenshots/gui_showcase.png
# Update pubspec.yaml: path: screenshots/gui_showcase.webp
```

CHANGELOG entry under whatever the next version is (likely 1.1.1):

> Convert `screenshots/gui_showcase.png` (801 KB) to lossless WebP
> (179 KB, −78%). Pixel-identical to the source PNG; pub.dev's
> screenshot pipeline is WebP-native via pana's `webpinfo` validator.
> Tarball drops from 835 KB to ~213 KB.

`cwebp` is provided by Homebrew's `webp` formula on macOS and `webp` package on Debian/Ubuntu — already installed on the maintainer's machine.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] `screenshots/gui_showcase.webp` exists, lossless WebP, ~179 KB, 1766×2062 dimensions preserved
- [ ] `screenshots/gui_showcase.png` removed from the repo
- [ ] `pubspec.yaml` `screenshots:` entry updated to point at the `.webp` path
- [ ] `dart pub publish --dry-run` reports a tarball under 250 KB
- [ ] CHANGELOG entry added under the new version
- [ ] No regression on the rendered pub.dev listing (verify after publish)
notes:
Converted via `cwebp -lossless -z 9 -m 6` on 2026-04-28.

Output: `screenshots/gui_showcase.webp`, 183,230 bytes (≈179 KB), VP8L, 1766×2062, alpha=1. Lossless ARGB; pixel-identical to the source PNG. Matches the 183 KB benchmark prediction in the design block.

Result vs. acceptance criteria:
- ✓ webp exists, ~179 KB, dimensions preserved
- ✓ PNG removed via `git rm`
- ✓ pubspec.yaml screenshots: path → .webp
- ✗ tarball under 250 KB → actually 309 KB. Still −63% from the 835 KB pre-conversion measurement, but above the 213 KB estimate. The gap is the example/ and example.md content that pana's tarball assembly now pulls in alongside the screenshot. Within pub.dev limits, no further action needed.
- ✓ CHANGELOG entry added under 1.1.2
- (post-publish verification deferred)

Landing under 1.1.2 (the previously prepared 1.1.1 was never published; pub.dev's latest is still 1.1.0).
close_reason: Converted gui_showcase.png (820,984 B) → gui_showcase.webp (183,230 B, −78%) via cwebp -lossless -z 9 -m 6. Lossless ARGB, dimensions preserved 1766×2062, alpha intact. pubspec.yaml screenshots path updated, PNG removed. Landing under 1.1.2.

title: chore(ntp): broaden NtpError::Unsynchronized diagnostic to cover stratum ceiling
description:
Once the validation path was extended to also reject `stratum >= 16`, the existing `Display` arm `"server reports unsynchronized clock (LI=3)"` was misleading — a stratum-16 rejection would surface that string and confuse anyone correlating logs to the wire.

**Fix:** Broaden to `"server reports unsynchronized clock (LI=3 or stratum >= 16)"`; rewrite the variant rustdoc to explain why both conditions collapse into one variant (identical caller semantics) and cross-reference `STRATUM_UNSYNCHRONIZED_FLOOR`.

**FFI impact:** None. The `From<NtpError> for NtsError` arm routes `Unsynchronized` through `NtsError::NtpProtocol(e.to_string())`, so the broadened string surfaces on the Dart side automatically — no FRB regen, no binding drift.

**Files:** `rust/src/nts/ntp.rs`

**Discovered from:** the stratum-16 ceiling work.
close_reason: Implemented in this session. `Display` for `NtpError::Unsynchronized` broadened to `"server reports unsynchronized clock (LI=3 or stratum >= 16)"`; variant rustdoc rewritten to cross-reference `STRATUM_UNSYNCHRONIZED_FLOOR`. No FFI churn — surface through `NtsError::NtpProtocol(e.to_string())` is automatic. Shipped in 1.1.0 / nts_rust 0.2.0.

title: chore(example): remove developer meta-commentary from example.md
description:
`example/example.md` (introduced in 1.0.4 to win the pub.dev Example tab via pana priority slot 2) contains an introductory paragraph that explains the *internal rationale* for the file's existence — specifically how pana's selection algorithm ranks files and why the `.md` form was added to outrank `example/lib/main.dart`. That commentary leaks release-engineering meta into the consumer-facing tab and should be replaced with a clean, neutral intro that simply describes what the snippet does and where to find the runnable counterpart and the GUI.

## Text to remove

The block at `example/example.md:7-11` (verbatim, current state):

> This file is the canonical Example tab content on pub.dev. The same
> source ships in the package as [`example/main.dart`](main.dart), which
> is the runnable Flutter target; the `.md` form is here so pub.dev's
> selection algorithm prefers it over the larger GUI showcase at
> [`example/lib/main.dart`](lib/main.dart).

Specifically:
- "This file is the canonical Example tab content on pub.dev." — meta-statement about the file's purpose on the platform; consumers do not need this.
- "the `.md` form is here so pub.dev's selection algorithm prefers it over the larger GUI showcase at `example/lib/main.dart`" — leaks pana priority logic.

## Text to keep / rework

Two consumer-relevant pieces are embedded inside the meta paragraph and must be preserved (re-stated without rationale):

1. The link/pointer to `example/main.dart` as the runnable Flutter target.
2. The link/pointer to `example/lib/main.dart` as the GUI showcase. (This is *also* covered by the existing `## Want the GUI?` section below the code block, so the in-paragraph link can be dropped without information loss.)

Suggested replacement of lines 7–11 — a single neutral sentence:

> The same code ships as a runnable Flutter target at [`example/main.dart`](main.dart).

Everything else in the file is already consumer-appropriate and stays untouched:
- Title (line 1) — keep.
- Description of the snippet (lines 3–5) — keep.
- The fenced ```dart block (lines 13–73) — keep verbatim; in-code comments are runtime/usage guidance, not pub-dev meta.
- The `## Want the GUI?` section (lines 75–90) — keep verbatim.

## Constraints

- Do **not** modify `example/main.dart`. The byte-for-byte equivalence between `example/main.dart` and the fenced ```dart block in `example.md` must continue to hold (this is a deliberate property — drift would break the executability guarantee documented in the runtime-comment chain).
- Do **not** bump `pubspec.yaml` or touch `CHANGELOG.md` as part of this issue. The file edit will ride with the next 1.0.5 release alongside any other queued changes; until then, 1.0.4's `example.md` stays live on pub.dev.
- Do **not** publish.

## Acceptance

- `example/example.md` no longer references "pub.dev's selection algorithm", "canonical Example tab content", or any other phrasing that exposes the pana priority rationale.
- The runnable-counterpart link to `example/main.dart` is preserved (reworded into a neutral one-liner is acceptable).
- The byte-for-byte equivalence between `example/main.dart` and the fenced ```dart block continues to hold (verifiable by extracting the code-fence block and diffing against `example/main.dart`).
- No `pubspec.yaml` or `CHANGELOG.md` edits in the same change.
acceptance_criteria:
- example/example.md no longer contains the "selection algorithm" / "canonical Example tab content" meta-commentary at lines 7-11.
- A neutral pointer to example/main.dart (runnable Flutter target) is retained.
- Fenced dart block in example.md remains byte-for-byte identical to example/main.dart.
- pubspec.yaml and CHANGELOG.md are NOT modified by this issue (deferred to 1.0.5 release).
- Title (line 1), description (lines 3-5), and `## Want the GUI?` section are unchanged.
close_reason: Meta-commentary about pub.dev rendering history removed from example/example.md, shipped in 1.0.5/1.0.6. The fenced sample is now the consumer-visible artefact only; rendering history lives in CHANGELOG.md and commit messages.

title: Validate Web/WASM platform tag for nts (FFI loader behaviour on Flutter Web)
description:
pana awarded the Web platform tag with the WASM-ready badge for nts 1.0.1:

  Platform support 20/20 -- Supports 6 of 6 possible platforms (..., Web)
  WASM-ready: This package is compatible with runtime wasm.

This is surprising for an FFI/native-assets crate where the Rust code is
the entire authentication surface. The Dart side likely compiles for
dart2js/dart2wasm because the FFI surface is behind conditional imports
(frb_generated.io.dart vs a stub), but actual runtime use of any nts API
on Web should fail at RustLib.init() since there is no native binary
to load.

Validate the Web compile + runtime behaviour:

1. Confirm pub.dev's tag is mechanical (compiles cleanly) versus functional
   (calls succeed). Likely the former.
2. Probe the actual error path: what does a consumer see if they call
   await RustLib.init() in a Flutter Web app build? Is it a clear
   UnsupportedError / FFI loader failure, or a confusing crash?
3. Decide between three remediation stances:
   a. Keep the tag, add a README "Platform support" subsection that
      explicitly notes Web is build-time only.
   b. Add a runtime guard in lib/src/nts.dart that throws
      UnsupportedError('nts requires native code; Web is not supported')
      before reaching the FFI layer, with a clear message.
   c. Drop the Web tag via pubspec platform declaration (would lose
      20/20 -> 16/20 platform points, so unlikely worth it).

Recommendation likely (a) + (b).

Acceptance:
- Documented runtime behaviour on Web (test harness or written probe).
- README clearly states the Web caveat.
- If (b) chosen, runtime guard exists with test coverage.
notes:
## Revert 2026-04-27: switched approach from runtime guard to explicit
## platform allow-list

The (a) + (b) approach landed in f366ab6 was reconsidered: it left pana
showing Web/WASM tags (technically incorrect), required a hand-authored
wrapper layer over every public entry point, and put consumers in a
position where their first signal of "this won't work" was an
UnsupportedError at runtime. The cleaner answer is to tell pub.dev the
truth at the metadata level and let the platform tags reflect reality.

## Changes in this revert commit

- pubspec.yaml: added top-level `platforms:` block listing only
  android / ios / macos / linux / windows. The schema key is at the
  top level (not under `flutter:`) -- that's the location pana reads
  for non-plugin packages, and `flutter.platforms` is not a valid
  pubspec schema key. Comment in the file records the protocol-level
  reasoning (raw TCP for NTS-KE on :4460, raw UDP for NTPv4 on :123,
  rustls + ring not targeting wasm32-unknown-unknown).

- lib/nts.dart: reverted to the export-only shape from before f366ab6.
  Re-exports RustLib directly from src/ffi/frb_generated.dart and the
  api functions (ntsQuery, ntsWarmCookies, greet) directly from their
  FFI files. No hand-authored wrapper class, no runtime guard, no
  divergence between the documented surface and the FRB-generated
  contract.

- lib/src/web_unsupported.dart: deleted.

- test/web_guard_test.dart: deleted.

- test/ffi_smoke_test.dart: restored to the pre-f366ab6 import shape;
  the `show RustLibApi` narrowing workaround is no longer needed
  because RustLib is once again re-exported directly from the same
  source the test imports.

- README.md: removed the "Platform support" section in its entirety
  (the table, the TCP/UDP browser-limitation prose, and the
  RustLib.initMock-as-Web-fallback guidance). README focuses strictly
  on native usage. The platform truth now lives in pub.dev's
  scorecard via the explicit allow-list.

## Validation

- fvm dart analyze .                  => "No issues found!"
- fvm flutter test (package)          => 5 passed (FRB smoke tests
                                         exercising RustLib.initMock
                                         through the restored direct
                                         re-export)
- cd example && fvm flutter analyze   => "No issues found!"
- cd example && fvm flutter test      => 31 passed; widget_test.dart
                                         drives RustLib.initMock via
                                         the same export path and the
                                         CLI's `RustLib.init(...)` +
                                         `RustLib.kDefaultExternal...`
                                         references compile unchanged.
- check_bindings drift gate semantics unaffected (its watched paths
  are lib/src/ffi + rust/src/frb_generated.rs; nothing under either
  was touched).

## Tradeoffs accepted

pana's platform-tag check awards points for the union of "Dart code
compiles for X" and "the package declares X", so this allow-list will
cause the Web/WASM tag points to drop on the next pub.dev rescore.
Score honesty preferred over score maximization.

The example app's `kIsWeb` fallback to `RustLib.initMock` continues to
work without source changes -- it never depended on the runtime guard,
just on `RustLib.initMock` being a no-op-on-web entry point, which it
still is.
close_reason: Replaced runtime UnsupportedError guard with explicit top-level pubspec platforms: allow-list (android/ios/macos/linux/windows). pub.dev scorecard will drop the Web/WASM tags on next pana rescore -- correct platform surface preferred over score maximization.

title: Narrow analyzer.exclude so local CI surfaces the same lints pana sees
description:
analysis_options.yaml currently excludes lib/src/ffi/** from analyser:

  analyzer:
    exclude:
      - lib/src/ffi/**

This silences dart analyze locally on the FRB-generated bindings, but pana
on pub.dev ignores the exclude directive when computing the static-analysis
score. The result was a 10-point silent drift between local CI green and
the live pana score (caught only by reading https://pub.dev/packages/nts/score).

Investigate scoping the exclude more tightly so local CI surfaces the same
public_member_api_docs lints pana sees:

Options:
  - Remove the exclude entirely. Drawback: noisy local output for FRB
    artefacts we cannot edit.
  - Narrow to lib/src/ffi/frb_generated.dart and lib/src/ffi/frb_generated.io.dart
    only, leaving lib/src/ffi/api/** under the analyser. The api/nts.dart file
    is the contract surface and is where the four missing-doc symbols live.
  - Move the exclude to errors: section with public_member_api_docs: ignore
    on the codegen files only -- preserves analyser coverage of those files
    for non-doc lints.

Acceptance:
- Local fvm dart analyze produces (at minimum) the same public_member_api_docs
  hits pana surfaces, OR a documented lint-budget rationale exists for any
  intentional gap.
- Drift-check (tool/check_bindings.dart) untouched in semantics.
- Depends on nts-DOCS (the Rust docstrings work) so we don't make the local
  output noisier than pana before fixing the underlying lints.
notes:
## Resolution 2026-04-27

Removed the `analyzer.exclude` block in `analysis_options.yaml` entirely. The dependency on `nts-o5w` (closed in commit 16b5ea7) had already added file-level `// ignore_for_file:` directives to the FRB-generated files via `tool/check_bindings.dart`'s `_lintIgnorePatches` table:

  - lib/src/ffi/api/nts.dart      : public_member_api_docs
  - lib/src/ffi/frb_generated.dart: public_member_api_docs, prefer_final_locals, prefer_const_constructors
  - lib/src/ffi/frb_generated.io.dart : public_member_api_docs
  - lib/src/ffi/frb_generated.web.dart: public_member_api_docs

`api/nts.freezed.dart` carries its own `// ignore_for_file: type=lint` (emitted by freezed itself). `api/simple.dart` has all public symbols documented and needs no doc-lint suppression.

Because pana on pub.dev honours `// ignore_for_file:` directives but not `analyzer.exclude`, the same files now lint identically locally and on pana, eliminating the silent drift the issue described.

## Validation

- fvm dart analyze . => "No issues found!"
- cd example && fvm flutter analyze => "No issues found!"
- tool/check_bindings.dart untouched (drift detection unchanged)
- analyzer.language settings (strict-casts/inference/raw-types) preserved
- linter.rules (public_member_api_docs, avoid_print, prefer_const_constructors, prefer_final_locals) preserved

The only change to the analyzer config is removing the four-line `exclude:` block and replacing it with an inline comment explaining the rationale (so future contributors don't re-add it without thinking).
close_reason: Removed lib/src/ffi/** analyzer exclude; file-level ignore_for_file directives (managed by tool/check_bindings.dart) provide pana-equivalent suppression. Local analyze parity with pana achieved.

title: Promote v1.0.0 git tag to a GitHub Release with notes
description:
The annotated tag `v1.0.0` (commit b0e17311) was pushed to origin during the pub.dev publication, but no GitHub Release object was created from it. Promote the tag to a Release so the package's release history surfaces in the GitHub repo sidebar and is consumable by downstream changelog tooling (release-tracking dashboards, dependabot release notes, etc.).

Steps:
1. github.com/nick-llewellyn/nts → Releases → "Draft a new release"
2. Choose existing tag: `v1.0.0`
3. Title: `v1.0.0 — Initial pub.dev release`
4. Body: paste the `CHANGELOG.md` 1.0.0 section verbatim (Markdown renders correctly on GitHub)
5. Mark as "Latest release"
6. Publish

Optionally automate this for future releases via a `softprops/action-gh-release` step in `.github/workflows/`, triggered on `push: tags: ['v*']`. Not in scope for this ticket — file as a follow-up if desired.

Purely cosmetic; does not affect the published pub.dev artifact (which is irrevocable for v1.0.0 anyway). Safe to defer indefinitely.

Cross-references:
- Tag SHA: 00d18fdfdf016612fd925c2d0ddd6ad3e2aac4da
- Target commit: b0e17311216f3858bca5ad32e35a5b9f5a36d55c
- pub.dev: https://pub.dev/packages/nts/versions/1.0.0
- CHANGELOG: CHANGELOG.md (1.0.0 section)
close_reason: Closing as won't-do. v1.0.0 was a public release and is shipped; promoting the tag to a GitHub Release is purely cosmetic and not worth tracking. v1.0.1 supersedes it as the active release going forward, and any future GitHub Release work will be tracked against the current tag rather than retroactively against v1.0.0.

title: Add local FRB bindings drift-detection script
description:
## Problem

CI's `Verify FRB bindings are in sync` job (in `.github/workflows/ci.yml`) regenerates bindings via `flutter_rust_bridge_codegen generate` and fails with a helpful `::error::FRB bindings drifted from rust/src/api/. Run 'flutter_rust_bridge_codegen generate' locally and commit the result.` message when `lib/src/ffi/` or `rust/src/frb_generated.rs` differs from the committed state.

There is currently no equivalent local check, so contributors only discover drift after pushing and waiting for CI (~8m33s based on the most recent run).

## Proposed solution

Add a small wrapper that mirrors the CI logic locally:

- Path: `tool/check_bindings.sh` (or `tool/check_bindings.dart` for parity with `example/tool/generate_icon.py` style)
- Behavior:
  1. Ensure `flutter_rust_bridge_codegen` is on PATH (fail with install hint pointing at the pinned `=2.12.0` version used by CI if not).
  2. Run `flutter_rust_bridge_codegen generate`.
  3. `git diff --exit-code -- lib/src/ffi rust/src/frb_generated.rs`.
  4. On drift: print the same `::error::` message CI uses (minus the GitHub Actions prefix) and exit non-zero.
  5. On success: print "FRB bindings are in sync" and exit 0.

## Acceptance

- Script exists, is executable (`chmod +x` if shell), and is documented in the README's "Tooling at a glance" table or under "Regenerate bindings".
- Running the script on a clean checkout exits 0.
- Manually editing a `rust/src/api/*.rs` file without regenerating, then running the script, exits non-zero with the drift message.
- CI workflow optionally simplified to call this script (so the drift-detection logic lives in one place), or kept inline if preferred for clarity.

## Notes

- The pinned codegen version in CI is `=2.12.0` — keep them in lockstep or extract to a single source of truth (e.g. a `.frb-version` file or top-of-script constant).
- Consider whether this should also verify the generated files end with a trailing newline / pass `dart format`, since CI's format step would otherwise re-flag freshly regenerated bindings.
close_reason: Implemented tool/check_bindings.dart: reads pinned FRB version from pubspec.yaml, verifies the local flutter_rust_bridge_codegen is on PATH and matches, runs `generate`, runs `dart format lib/src/ffi`, then `git diff --exit-code -- lib/src/ffi rust/src/frb_generated.rs`. Emits the same drift message CI uses (with the `::error::` prefix only when GITHUB_ACTIONS is set) and exits 1 on drift. Simplified .github/workflows/ci.yml's `Verify FRB bindings are in sync` job to `dart run tool/check_bindings.dart` (single source of truth). README updated with tool entry + `Verify bindings are in sync` section. Verified locally: clean run exits 0; adding an exported fn to rust/src/api/simple.rs without regenerating produces exit=1 with the expected diff stat + error message.

title: chore(example/icons): regenerate launcher PNGs against new indigo brand palette
description:
The brand palette flipped from `#0066FF` (Labrys blue) to `#3F51B5` (Material Indigo 500) prior to the standalone-repo migration. The vector source (`example/assets/icon/source.svg`) and rasterizer (`example/tool/generate_icon.py`) were updated, but the committed launcher PNGs (`example/assets/icon/icon.png`, `icon_background.png`, `icon_foreground.png` and the platform-density siblings under `example/android/app/src/main/res/`) still bake the old hex.

Steps:
1. `pip install Pillow` (or use a uv ad-hoc env)
2. `cd example && python tool/generate_icon.py`
3. `dart run flutter_launcher_icons`
4. Eyeball the output on a device + simulator at small sizes
5. Commit the regenerated PNGs

No functional impact — the app still installs and runs; only the visual brand is mismatched on the launcher tile until this is run.
close_reason: Regenerated example launcher PNGs from the current SVG/Python source against the indigo brand palette (#3F51B5 plate, #1A237E hands). Workflow: created an isolated Pillow venv per the script's docstring (/tmp/icongen), ran example/tool/generate_icon.py to refresh the three master 1024² PNGs in example/assets/icon/, then `dart run flutter_launcher_icons` (v0.14.4) to fan out to platform-specific variants. 47 binary files updated total: 3 masters, 15 Android mipmap/drawable PNGs, 21 iOS Icon-App-*.png, 7 macOS app_icon_*.png, app_icon.ico (Windows). Programmatic verification with PIL across all sizes (16² → 1024²) confirms #3F51B5 corner pixels and #1A237E centers, including the actool-compiled Assets.car and the legacy AppIcon.icns inside example/build/macos/Build/Products/Debug/NTS.app/Contents/Resources/. `flutter analyze` on example/ stayed clean. Visual eyeball: Android launcher tile confirmed indigo on device. macOS Dock continued showing the old blue tile — confirmed via assetutil + iconutil that this is a system-level cache (LaunchServices/iconservices/Dock), NOT stale assets in the bundle. Documented the lsregister -f / touch / iconservices-cache reset escalation path in chat for whoever revisits this. Filed nts-nvv-style follow-up unnecessary: the on-disk truth is correct everywhere; the Dock cache is a per-machine concern.

title: Establish #[expect(lint, reason)] convention; migrate any #[allow] sites
description:
## Context

Rust 1.81 stabilised `#[expect(lint, reason = "...")]` as the
recommended form of localised lint suppression. Unlike `#[allow]`,
which silently suppresses a lint regardless of whether the lint
would actually fire, `#[expect]` requires the lint to fire — if a
later refactor resolves the issue, the compiler emits
`lint_reasons` warning that the suppression is now dead and can be
removed.

`ntpd-rs ntp-proto/src/{nts/mod.rs, nts/messages.rs, server.rs}`
already use this form pervasively, e.g.:

```rust
#[cfg(test)]
#[expect(
    clippy::too_many_lines,
    reason = "Long tests are not really a big problem"
)]
mod tests { ... }
```

Our codebase carries **zero** `#[allow(...)]` sites in hand-written
code today — verified by `grep -rn "#\[allow" rust/src/`. The only
hit is a comment in `rust/src/nts/mod.rs` referencing the word
"allow". So the migration scope of "swap existing `#[allow]` for
`#[expect]`" is empty at the moment.

The value of this ticket is therefore **forward-looking**: once the
clippy lint set from the Linting and Safety Infrastructure ticket
is activated, the triage pass will surface candidate suppression
sites. This ticket establishes the convention so those sites land as
`#[expect(..., reason = "...")]` from day one rather than
accumulating as `#[allow]`s that nobody knows are stale.

## Recommendation

1. **Document the convention.** Add a section to `DEVELOPMENT.md`
   under `## Lint suppression policy` (or equivalent heading) with
   the rule:
   - **Default:** fix the lint at the call site.
   - **Local suppression:** use `#[expect(lint_name, reason = "<sentence
     explaining why the lint is wrong here and what would change
     that>")]`. Never `#[allow]`.
   - **Crate-wide suppression:** drop the lint from
     `[lints.clippy]` in `rust/Cargo.toml` with an inline comment
     explaining the decision. Per-call-site `#[expect]` is preferred
     unless the lint is wrong everywhere.

2. **Reusable boilerplate stanza** for the doc:

   ```rust
   #[expect(
       clippy::too_many_lines,
       reason = "Test bodies are intentionally long to exercise the
                 full positive/negative input matrix; splitting would
                 obscure the relationship between cases."
   )]
   ```

3. **Audit + migrate (currently empty).** Run
   `grep -rn "#\[allow" rust/src/` at implementation time. Any
   `#[allow]` sites that have appeared since this ticket was filed
   (e.g. from interim work between filing and execution) get
   converted to `#[expect]`. Today this audit is expected to return
   zero hits.

4. **Forbid future `#[allow]` introductions via reviewer policy.**
   Optionally enforce with a `lints.rust` entry:
   ```toml
   # in rust/Cargo.toml [lints.clippy]
   # No clippy lint corresponds to "ban #[allow]"; this is purely a
   # reviewer-convention item. A grep-based pre-commit hook is
   # possible but probably overkill — the cost of one stale
   # suppression slipping through is low.
   ```

   I.e. don't burn engineering effort on tooling for a convention
   the team can hold via review.

## Adaptation notes (sync / sans-IO)

- No code shape changes. This is convention + documentation only.
- The `reason = "..."` field requires Rust 1.81+. If the
  Linting/Safety Infrastructure ticket lands an MSRV below 1.81, this
  ticket is blocked until the MSRV rises. Per ntpd-rs's example,
  1.81 is the floor for the `reason` field to be respected.

## Acceptance criteria

- `DEVELOPMENT.md` carries a "Lint suppression policy" section
  documenting `#[expect(..., reason = "...")]` as the canonical form.
- Any `#[allow(...)]` sites that exist at implementation time (other
  than the singleton on `frb_generated` from the lint-infra ticket)
  are migrated to `#[expect]` with non-trivial `reason` strings.
- The `frb_generated` `#[allow(unsafe_code)]` from the lint-infra
  ticket is acceptable as-is — `#[expect(unsafe_code)]` would also
  work but the FRB-generated content evolves between regenerations
  so the suppression is durable, not temporary.
- `cargo clippy --manifest-path rust/Cargo.toml -- -D warnings` is
  clean.
- Reviewer reference checklist updated (or pull-request template, if
  the project has one) to flag `#[allow]` introductions for
  conversion to `#[expect]`.

## Cross-references

- Reference: `ntpd-rs ntp-proto/src/nts/messages.rs:517` and
  `ntp-proto/src/server.rs:227,490` (v1.7.2) for example `#[expect]`
  usage with `reason` field.
- Dependent on: the Linting and Safety Infrastructure ticket
  (lint-set activation surfaces the candidate suppression sites).
- Rust 1.81 stabilisation reference:
  <https://blog.rust-lang.org/2024/09/05/Rust-1.81.0.html#new-expect-attribute>.

title: Extract rust/src/api/nts.rs test module into sibling tests.rs (5305-line file split ~50/50 impl/tests)
description:
## Context

`rust/src/api/nts.rs` is 5,305 lines as of feat/3.1.0-warm-cookies-singleflight (PR #37):

- Lines 1-2545: impl (top-level functions, NtsClient, SessionTable + singleflight machinery, conversion glue, FRB-bound entry points)
- Lines 2546-5305: `#[cfg(test)] mod tests { ... }` (BoundedRelease helper, make_test_session helpers, ~50 unit tests covering checkout / warm_cookies / trust-state / etc.)

The test mass slightly outweighs the impl. Editor navigation, code review (~140-line jump-to-anchor for Copilot inline comments on the warm_cookies tests), and `git diff` readability all suffer.

## Recommendation

Extract the test module into a sibling submodule file using the standard Rust 2018+ module layout (option 2 from the chat 2026-05-12). No path changes, no visibility widening, no CI impact.

```
src/api/nts.rs        ← impl + `#[cfg(test)] mod tests;`
src/api/nts/tests.rs  ← the moved-out test module
```

Mechanics:

1. Create `rust/src/api/nts/tests.rs`.
2. Move every line currently inside `#[cfg(test)] mod tests { ... }` (lines 2547-5304, the body only) verbatim into the new file. Drop the `mod tests {` and matching `}` lines.
3. Replace the inline body in `nts.rs` with a single declaration:

   ```rust
   #[cfg(test)]
   mod tests;
   ```

4. Confirm `use super::*;` already sits at the top of the moved block (it does today as of 9e16626).

Properties preserved:

- The moved file is still a child module of `crate::api::nts`, so it still sees every private impl item including `SessionTable::install` (`#[cfg(test)]`), `next_session_generation`, `session_key`, `make_test_session{,_with_cookies}`, `BoundedRelease`, `await_singleflight_state`.
- Test paths unchanged: `cargo test --lib api::nts::tests::warm_cookies_*` still resolves the same set.
- No `Cargo.toml` change, no test discovery change, no FRB regen, no Dart-side change.
- Coverage delta should be zero (line identity moves, line content does not).

## Optional follow-on (do not bundle here)

If the moved `tests.rs` is itself uncomfortably large (it will be ~2,750 lines), consider a second pass that splits it by topic into a sub-tree under `src/api/nts/tests/`:

- `src/api/nts/tests/mod.rs` (re-exports + helper `mod common;` declaration)
- `src/api/nts/tests/common.rs` (BoundedRelease, make_test_session, await_singleflight_state)
- `src/api/nts/tests/checkout.rs`
- `src/api/nts/tests/warm_cookies.rs`
- `src/api/nts/tests/handshake_slot.rs`
- `src/api/nts/tests/trust_state.rs`
- `src/api/nts/tests/establish_session.rs`
- ... etc.

That second pass requires deciding how to bucket each test, so it's a larger ticket; this issue is scoped to the single-file extraction only.

## Acceptance criteria

- `rust/src/api/nts.rs` ends with `#[cfg(test)] mod tests;` and contains no inline test code.
- `rust/src/api/nts/tests.rs` exists and contains the moved test module body.
- `cargo test --lib` reports the same passing test count as before (currently 176).
- `cargo clippy --lib --tests -- -D warnings` clean.
- `tool/check_bindings.dart` exits 0 (FRB orphan-detection comment unchanged: the moved tests are still `#[cfg(test)]`-gated and still reference the same private functions, so the FRB parser sees the same set of pub/non-pub functions in `nts.rs`).
- No CHANGELOG entry needed (internal layout refactor, no observable change).

## Provenance

Chat session 2026-05-12 (post-PR-#37); user agreed to file as a follow-up rather than bundle into PR #37 since the PR is already in review and any move-only commit would re-trigger every CI check for zero behaviour change.

title: Add IdentityAead test helper for framing-only tests in ntp.rs
description:
## Context

Several tests in `rust/src/nts/ntp.rs::tests` exercise
extension-field framing, padding, and slot-ordering behaviour but
have to construct a real AEAD instance (`fresh_keys()` →
`AeadKey::SivCmac256`) and dispatch the real AES-SIV-CMAC-256
deterministic-IV machinery just to get a working `siv_seal` / `siv_open`
pair. The framing assertions then have to either:

- Take the resulting ciphertext as opaque bytes and only assert on
  the surrounding framing, or
- Hard-code AEAD-specific output sizes (16-byte SIV tag, 16-byte
  nonce) into the assertions, coupling framing-test correctness to
  AEAD-implementation correctness.

`ntpd-rs ntp-proto/src/packet/crypto.rs::IdentityCipher` (lines 326-
384 in v1.7.2) is a `#[cfg(test)] pub struct IdentityCipher` that
implements the same `Cipher` trait as `AesSivCmac256` but performs
**no encryption**: `encrypt` copies plaintext through unchanged and
prepends a deterministic nonce of caller-specified length; `decrypt`
returns the ciphertext slice verbatim. Their framing-only tests
(e.g. `nonce_padding`, `serialize_untrusted_fields`,
`deserialize_without_cipher`) use `IdentityCipher::new(11)` to
decouple "are the bytes laid out correctly" from "does the AEAD
round-trip correctly".

We don't have this helper. Several follow-up tickets (in particular
`test_undersized_ef_in_encrypted_data` from the regression-corpus
ticket) need it to avoid coupling framing-regression coverage to
real-AEAD correctness.

## Recommendation

Add a `#[cfg(test)] pub(crate) mod test_helpers` (or extend the
existing `tests` module's helpers if scope-locality is preferred) in
`rust/src/nts/aead.rs`:

```text
#[cfg(test)]
pub(crate) struct IdentityAead {
    nonce_len: usize,
}

impl IdentityAead {
    pub(crate) fn new(nonce_len: usize) -> Self { Self { nonce_len } }

    pub(crate) fn seal(
        &self, _ad: &[&[u8]], plaintext: &[u8],
    ) -> Result<Vec<u8>, AeadError> {
        // nonce = (0..nonce_len as u8).collect(); body = plaintext.
        // No tag, no encryption — pure copy.
    }

    pub(crate) fn open(
        &self, _ad: &[&[u8]], nonce: &[u8], ciphertext: &[u8],
    ) -> Result<Vec<u8>, AeadError> {
        // Sanity-check nonce.len() == self.nonce_len; return
        // ciphertext.to_vec().
    }
}
```

API shape matches our `siv_seal` / `siv_open` so tests can call into
it as a drop-in replacement at the framing level. Do **not** wire
this into the production `AeadKey` enum — keep it strictly behind
`#[cfg(test)]` to ensure it can never reach a release build even via
a feature-flag misuse.

If callers need uniform dispatch (e.g. a function generic over both
real AEAD and IdentityAead), introduce a private test trait
`TestCipher` rather than expanding the `AeadKey` enum.

## Adaptation notes (sync / sans-IO)

- ntpd-rs's `IdentityCipher` implements their `Cipher` trait, which
  expects in-place buffer mutation. Ours is value-in/value-out (per
  `siv_seal`/`siv_open` signatures). The shape difference makes the
  port a near-rewrite, not a copy.
- Keep the helper's nonce a deterministic sequence (`0..nonce_len as
  u8`) so framing assertions can hard-code expected nonce bytes
  without depending on RNG.
- `pub(crate)` rather than `pub`: framing tests in `ntp.rs` need
  cross-module access, but external callers must not.

## Acceptance criteria

- `IdentityAead` exists under `#[cfg(test)]` in
  `rust/src/nts/aead.rs`.
- One or two existing framing tests in `rust/src/nts/ntp.rs::tests`
  are migrated to use `IdentityAead` (suggest: tests that currently
  hard-code 16-byte tag or 16-byte nonce lengths in assertions) to
  demonstrate the helper works as intended.
- The migrated tests still pass.
- `cargo test --manifest-path rust/Cargo.toml` is green.
- `cargo clippy --manifest-path rust/Cargo.toml -- -D warnings` is
  clean.
- `IdentityAead` is not reachable from any non-test path — confirm by
  `cargo check --manifest-path rust/Cargo.toml --release` succeeding
  without referring to the type.

title: warm_cookies_with derives count via post-insert lookup with masking unwrap_or(0); compute from session.cookies_remaining() before move
description:
## Context

In `SessionTable::warm_cookies_with` (rust/src/api/nts.rs:1820-1826),
the leader path computes the freshly-installed cookie count by
inserting the session, then re-reading it back out:

```rust
let count = {
    let mut g = self.map.lock().expect("session table poisoned");
    g.insert(key.clone(), session);
    g.get(&key)
        .map(|s| s.cookies_remaining() as u32)
        .unwrap_or(0)
};
```

Two problems with this shape (Copilot review on PR #37,
https://github.com/nick-llewellyn/nts/pull/37#discussion_r3222428622):

1. The `unwrap_or(0)` masks an invariant violation. Under
   `HashMap` semantics, a `get` immediately following an
   `insert` *under the same lock* with the same key cannot
   return `None`. If it ever did, the leader would silently
   return `Ok((0, ...))` to the caller — a misleading
   "warm succeeded with 0 cookies" outcome that the
   pre-handshake `cookies_remaining() == 0` guard above was
   specifically added to prevent surfacing.
2. The lookup is also redundant: `session.cookies_remaining()`
   is already known immediately before the `g.insert` (the
   surrounding `if session.cookies_remaining() == 0 { ... }`
   guard reads the same value).

## Recommendation

Compute `count` from `session.cookies_remaining()` *before*
moving `session` into the map. Avoids both the post-insert
lookup and the silent-fallback masking.

```rust
let count = session.cookies_remaining() as u32;
self.map.lock().expect("session table poisoned").insert(key.clone(), session);
```

The "atomically install + capture count under the map lock"
comment can be deleted because the count is no longer derived
from the post-install state. The remaining race the comment
named (a concurrent `checkout` popping cookies between insert
and snapshot) is a feature for the cookie-pool semantics, not
something `warm_cookies` needs to defend against — the
documented contract is "fresh-cookie count from the just-run
handshake," not "cookies still in the pool when we return."

## Acceptance criteria

- `warm_cookies_with` returns `count` derived from
  `session.cookies_remaining()` before the move.
- The post-insert lookup is removed.
- All four `warm_cookies_*` tests added in
  `feat/3.1.0-warm-cookies-singleflight` continue to pass.

## Provenance

Copilot review on PR #37
(https://github.com/nick-llewellyn/nts/pull/37#discussion_r3222428622).
close_reason: Shipped by feat/3.1.0-warm-cookies-singleflight (PR #37 follow-up): warm_cookies_with now derives count = session.cookies_remaining() as u32 *before* moving session into the map. The post-insert g.get(&key).map(...).unwrap_or(0) is replaced with a direct cookies_remaining() snapshot, which (a) removes the unwrap_or(0) fallback that would have masked an invariant break as a misleading 'warm succeeded with 0 cookies' outcome, and (b) avoids a redundant HashMap lookup. All 4 warm_cookies_* singleflight tests still pass; cargo test --lib 173/173; clippy clean; flutter analyze + test 46/46; FRB drift check exits 0 (no API surface change, only function-body refactor).

title: Optional AES-SIV-CMAC-512 (IANA AEAD ID 17) support
description:
## Context

`rust/src/nts/records.rs::aead` advertises two AEAD algorithms:

```rust
pub const AES_SIV_CMAC_256: u16 = 15;   // RFC 8915 mandatory
pub const AES_128_GCM_SIV:  u16 = 30;   // RFC 8452
```

The IANA AEAD registry also lists `AES_SIV_CMAC_384 = 16` and
`AES_SIV_CMAC_512 = 17`. The 512-bit variant is supported directly by
the `aes-siv` crate already in our dep tree (via its `Aes256SivAead`
type with the longer key) and is the second-most-commonly-offered
algorithm after CMAC-256 in the deployed NTS server ecosystem (e.g.
`ntpd-rs`-backed deployments).

Today no public NTS-KE server prefers CMAC-512 over CMAC-256 in
practice — they offer both and the client picks. So this is a
future-proofing change, not a deployment-gap fix. Worth filing so it
doesn't get lost; not worth dropping P2 work for.

## Recommendation

1. Add `pub const AES_SIV_CMAC_512: u16 = 17;` to the `aead` module
   in `records.rs`.
2. Add `SivKey512 { bytes: [u8; 64] }` (or extend `SivKey` to a const
   generic `SivKey<const N: usize>`) and a `siv_seal_512` /
   `siv_open_512` pair in `aead.rs`. Const-generic shape is cleaner;
   pick whichever stays consistent with the rest of the module.
3. Extend the `AeadKey` enum with `SivCmac512(...)` and update the
   static dispatch in `siv_seal` / `siv_open` callers.
4. Extend `ke.rs::aead_key_len` and `exporter_context`'s caller to
   handle the new ID; update the offered-algorithms list in
   `build_request`.
5. Coordinate with ticket `nts-9oi` (which already calls out the
   loose coupling between the offered list, key-length table, and
   `AeadKey` constructor). This change expands that surface area, so
   either land `nts-9oi`'s tightening first or fold this ticket's
   work into the same PR.

## Out of scope

- `AES_SIV_CMAC_384` (ID 16). Rare in deployment, no clear demand,
  and the 384-bit `aes-siv` variant has additional library
  considerations. File separately if a real need surfaces.

title: Synchronous diagnostics dartdoc (ntsDnsPoolStats / ntsTrustStatus / NtsClient sync methods) should call out the RustLib.init() precondition
description:
## Context

`ntsDnsPoolStats()` and `ntsTrustStatus()` in `lib/src/api/nts.dart`
are documented as "Synchronous (no future / isolate hop): backed by
[atomic loads], cheap enough to call from a UI poll loop."

That is accurate, but both calls still dispatch through
`RustLib.instance.api.crateApiNts*`, which means they share the same
"`await RustLib.init()` must have completed first" precondition as
the rest of the public surface. Calling either before init throws
from FRB's dispatcher with a `StateError`, not an `NtsError`.

The README does say "await `RustLib.init()` once before any other
call," so the precondition is documented at the top level. But the
per-function dartdoc on `ntsDnsPoolStats` and `ntsTrustStatus`
emphasises the "synchronous, cheap" aspect without mentioning the
init dependency. A reader who skims only the per-function dartdoc
could reasonably conclude these are pure local functions safe to
call from anywhere.

## Why this matters

A common pattern is to wire a "diagnostics" panel that polls
`ntsDnsPoolStats()` from a `Timer.periodic`. If that panel mounts
before the bootstrap path runs `await RustLib.init()` (e.g. a route
that opens before the app's main bootstrap completes), the first
poll throws an unhelpful `StateError` from inside FRB rather than
the expected zero-value snapshot.

## Recommendation

Add a one-line "Initialization" note to the dartdoc on
`ntsDnsPoolStats` and `ntsTrustStatus` (and to the `NtsClient`
synchronous methods `invalidate`, `clear`, and the `trustMode`
getter, which share the same precondition). Match the existing
"Initialization" note already present on `NtsClient` (lines 244-252).

Cross-link to the library-level dartdoc on `package:nts/nts.dart`
which already explains the `RustLib.init()` requirement.

## Acceptance criteria

- Each synchronous public function in `lib/src/api/nts.dart` has a
  one-line precondition note pointing at `RustLib.init()`.
- No code changes; doc-only.

## Provenance

Code review 2026-05-11; Dart review finding (init precondition for
synchronous diagnostics).
close_reason: Closed by chore/3.1.0-docs-polish (this PR). PR #34 added the RustLib.init() precondition note to ntsDnsPoolStats() and ntsTrustStatus(). This PR extends the same note to the three NtsClient sync methods identified in the original ticket: NtsClient.invalidate, NtsClient.clear, and the NtsClient.trustMode getter.

title: trust_state default_backend is overwrite-on-store; add cumulative per-backend counters for dashboard trend visibility
description:
## Context

`ProcessTrustState::record_default_backend` in
`rust/src/nts/trust_state.rs:88-97` is a plain `Relaxed` store, so
the most recent default-singleton handshake wins. The snapshot dartdoc
on `defaultClientBackend` does say "most recently resolved to," so the
contract is honest.

## Why this matters

Operators reading the field on a dashboard might mistake it for a
"current state" signal rather than an event-driven one. Concrete
mis-read:

- Singleton handshake under platform succeeds → field reads `platform`.
- Later singleton handshake's `build_with_native_verifier` fails →
  field reads `webpkiRoots` permanently until the next platform-success
  handshake.

A dashboard panel labelled "Trust backend in use" would imply a state
that is actually a stale event marker.

## Recommendation

Add cumulative per-backend counters alongside the existing
"most-recent" pointer:

```rust
pub(crate) struct ProcessTrustState {
    default_backend: AtomicU8,                  // most-recent (existing)
    default_backend_platform_count: AtomicU64,  // new
    default_backend_hybrid_count: AtomicU64,    // new
    default_backend_webpki_count: AtomicU64,    // new
    android_platform_init_succeeded: AtomicBool,
    android_hybrid_fallback_count: AtomicU64,
}
```

Every `record_default_backend(b)` bumps the corresponding counter.
The snapshot returned by `nts_trust_status` exposes all four numbers
so a dashboard can render either:

- "Most recent backend: X" (existing field, unchanged shape).
- "Trend: P platform, H hybrid, W webpki across N total handshakes"
  (new fields).

This is purely additive — no existing field changes meaning, and the
existing `defaultClientBackend` Dart dartdoc continues to be accurate.

## Acceptance criteria

- New counters present on `TrustStateSnapshot` and the FFI-mapped
  `NtsTrustStatus`.
- Counter increments are tested in `trust_state.rs::tests` for each
  backend variant.
- Dart dartdoc on the new fields documents that they are cumulative
  process-wide counters with the same per-counter monotonicity
  contract as `androidHybridFallbackCount`.

## Provenance

Code review 2026-05-11; finding 5.5.

title: HybridVerifier warn-log fires before knowing the fallback chain is acceptable; misaligned with bumped counter
description:
## Context

`HybridVerifier::verify_server_cert` in `rust/src/nts/hybrid_verifier.rs`
emits a `log::warn!` **before** calling the fallback verifier:

- Lines 158-161 (Revoked path, "retrying with webpki-roots").
- Lines 182-186 (JNI-marker path, "retrying with webpki-roots").

The fallback `verify_server_cert` is called immediately after; if the
fallback also rejects the chain (e.g. webpki-roots does not include
the issuer either), the warn line implies "fell back successfully"
even though the chain ultimately fails.

The actual `record_fallback` call (line 177 / line 196) is correctly
gated on `result.is_ok()` so the **counter** is accurate. The **log**
is not.

## Why this matters

Operators triaging a deployment by tailing logs will see warn lines
that overcount actual fallbacks. Worse, when correlated with the
`androidHybridFallbackCount` counter exposed via `nts_trust_status`,
the two numbers will disagree (warn count > counter value), which
can mislead a reader into thinking the counter is broken.

## Recommendation

Move both `log::warn!` calls to fire **after** `result.is_ok()`,
co-located with the existing `self.record_fallback();` call:

```rust
if result.is_ok() {
    log::warn!(
        target: "nts::hybrid_verifier",
        "platform verifier reported Revoked for {host}; \
         webpki-roots fallback succeeded (likely missing OCSP AIA, \
         e.g. Let's Encrypt R12)",
    );
    self.record_fallback();
}
```

This keeps the warn signal aligned with the counter signal so
`(grep -c WARN nts::hybrid_verifier) == nts_trust_status().android_hybrid_fallback_count`
across a process lifetime.

## Acceptance criteria

- A test (or a careful manual trace) confirms that a chain rejected
  by both platform AND fallback produces no `WARN nts::hybrid_verifier`
  line, only the underlying webpki rejection error.
- A chain accepted by the fallback produces exactly one warn line
  per accept.

## Provenance

Code review 2026-05-11; finding 5.2.

title: NTS-KE record validation diagnostics: log unknown non-critical, distinguish empty AEAD body, log non-critical Error records
description:
## Context

Three small diagnostic improvements in `validate_response`
(`rust/src/nts/ke.rs:518-608`) and the surrounding record-handling
code. None affects correctness — all are observability nudges that
help operators triage non-conforming servers without changing the
shape of any error.

## 7a — Log unknown non-critical records

`validate_response` accepts any unknown record with `critical = false`
and silently drops it (RFC 8915 §4.1.4 mandates ignoring; that's
correct). But the code never logs the drop.

Adding a `log::debug!` listing the record type and body length would
help operators detect a server that starts emitting a new record
type over time without affecting the protocol behaviour.

## 7b — Distinguish empty AEAD-Algorithm body from missing record

Currently:

```rust
let (aead_critical, aead_id) = records
    .iter()
    .find_map(|r| match &r.kind {
        RecordKind::AeadAlgorithm(v) => v.first().copied().map(|id| (r.critical, id)),
        _ => None,
    })
    .ok_or(KeError::MissingAead)?;
```

If the server returns `RecordKind::AeadAlgorithm(vec![])` (an empty
body), `v.first().copied()` returns `None`, the outer `find_map`
collapses, and we report `KeError::MissingAead` ("response missing
AEAD Algorithm record") — but the record was present, just empty.

RFC 8915 §4.1.5 actually allows an empty body as a server-side
refusal signal. A more accurate diagnostic would be a new
`KeError::AeadNegotiationRefused` variant.

## 7c — Log non-critical Error records

`validate_response` returns `ServerError(code)` for any
`RecordKind::Error`, regardless of the critical bit (lines 523-526).
This is fail-safe (always honour an Error record), but RFC 8915
§4.1.5 requires the Error record to be Critical. A non-critical
Error record is a protocol violation; silently honouring it masks
the violation.

A `log::warn!` when `!r.critical && matches!(r.kind, Error(_))` would
let operators detect non-conforming servers without changing the
error shape.

## Recommendation

Either land these as three small commits in one PR, or split into
three sub-issues. None of them changes the public API.

## Acceptance criteria

- 7a: `log::debug!` emitted for each unknown non-critical record,
  including the record type and body length.
- 7b: Empty-body `AeadAlgorithm` record produces a distinct error
  variant from a fully missing `AeadAlgorithm` record.
- 7c: `log::warn!` emitted when an `Error` record is received without
  the critical bit set; the existing `ServerError(code)` return is
  unchanged.

## Provenance

Code review 2026-05-11; findings 3.1, 3.3, 3.4 (combined).

title: Update dependency-review-action to resolve Node.js 20 deprecation
description:
CI emits the Node.js 20 deprecation warning:

> Node.js 20 is deprecated. The following actions target Node.js 20 but are being forced to run on Node.js 24: actions/dependency-review-action@v4.9.0.

## Current state (verified 2026-04-30)

- Pin in `.github/workflows/ci.yml`: `actions/dependency-review-action@v4.9.0` (latest stable, released 2026-03-03).
- `action.yml` at tag `v4.9.0` declares `runs.using: 'node20'`.
- `action.yml` on `main` branch ALSO declares `runs.using: 'node20'`. No Node24-targeting release exists.
- Therefore, no version bump can resolve the warning today; this is upstream-blocked.

## Target version

Not yet released. Upstream PR actions/dependency-review-action#1084 ("Update Node.js runtime from 20 to 24") flips `runs.using` to `node24` and bumps `@types/node` to ^24. Open since 2026-04-08, not yet merged. Resolution requires upstream to merge and cut a tagged release (likely v4.10.0 or v5.0.0).

## File to be modified

`.github/workflows/ci.yml` (single occurrence: the `dependency-review` job's `uses:` pin).

## Plan when upstream lands

1. Bump pin from `actions/dependency-review-action@v4.9.0` to the new Node24 tag.
2. Verify a fresh CI run no longer emits the "forced to run on Node.js 24" warning.
3. Confirm `dependency-review` job exit-code/behaviour unchanged.

## Impact assessment

Stdout-only warning. The action continues to function correctly under the runner's Node24 shim. No GitHub annotation, no exit-code change, no behavioural impact. Cosmetic CI log noise only — same shape as nts-qc8.

## Deadline guidance

GitHub's runner default flips to Node24 on 2026-06-02; full Node20 removal is "later in fall 2026". If upstream PR #1084 has not merged by 2026-09-01, escalate to either pinning a fork or accepting the warning as documented benign noise until removal forces upstream's hand.
close_reason: Bumped pin from v4.9.0 -> v5.0.0 in .github/workflows/ci.yml. Upstream PR #1084 (Update Node.js runtime from 20 to 24) merged 2026-05-08 and shipped as v5.0.0 the same day. action.yml diff is single-line (using: node20 -> using: node24); inputs/outputs unchanged; sole behavioural diff in the changelog is a fix for patched-version display when advisories use non-strict semver ranges (e.g. Maven beta versions). Removed the FORCE_JAVASCRIPT_ACTIONS_TO_NODE24 env opt-in that was added as a stopgap and the now-stale comment block describing the upstream-blocked state.

title: Watch flutter_rust_bridge releases past 2.12.0; bump pin to keep 40/40 pana dependency score
description:
pubspec.yaml pins flutter_rust_bridge to the exact version 2.12.0 (no caret).
pana 1.0.1 currently reports this as 40/40 because Compatible == Latest ==
2.12.0. The moment upstream ships 2.12.1 (or any later), the
"All package dependencies are supported in the latest version" sub-check
will drop from 10/10 to 0/10, costing us 10 pana points (160 -> 150 again).

The exact pin is intentional: FRB has historically shipped breaking changes
across patch versions, and tool/check_bindings.dart enforces that the
codegen output matches the committed bindings byte-for-byte. Bumping FRB
without regenerating bindings will break the drift check; bumping with
regen requires verifying the API surface and cargo-side compatibility.

Workflow when upstream ships >2.12.0:

1. Update flutter_rust_bridge in pubspec.yaml.
2. Update flutter_rust_bridge_codegen dev_dependency match.
3. Update Cargo.toml to match major.minor.
4. cargo update -p flutter_rust_bridge.
5. fvm dart run flutter_rust_bridge_codegen generate.
6. Inspect diff in lib/src/ffi/** and rust/src/frb_generated.rs.
7. fvm flutter test, melos run analyze.
8. Run tool/check_bindings.dart locally.
9. Update CHANGELOG.md.
10. Bump nts version (likely patch).

Watch trigger: subscribe to flutter_rust_bridge GitHub releases or poll
https://pub.dev/api/packages/flutter_rust_bridge weekly.

Acceptance:
- Stays open, polled at session boundaries.
- Closed when next FRB version is published and we have either bumped or
  documented why we cannot.

title: Track dorny/paths-filter Node 20 -> Node 24 migration
description:
GitHub Actions runner emits a deprecation warning for dorny/paths-filter@v3 because its action.yml declares 'runs.using: node20'. GitHub will force Node 24 on 2026-06-02 and remove Node 20 from runners on 2026-09-16.

Reference: https://github.blog/changelog/2025-09-19-deprecation-of-node-20-on-github-actions-runners/

## Context

Adopted dorny/paths-filter@v3 in commit c3facae (2026-04-27) for per-area job gating. The action is pure JavaScript with no native bindings, so it is likely to function unchanged under Node 24 once GitHub auto-promotes it on 2026-06-02. Latest upstream tag at time of filing is v3.0.2.

This is a separate concern from nts-nvv / nts-qc8, which track DEP0040 punycode warnings inside Swatinem/rust-cache bundled npm dependencies (a different action, different warning class).

## Plan

1. Now: add an early opt-in via env FORCE_JAVASCRIPT_ACTIONS_TO_NODE24=true on the changes job to validate that paths-filter@v3 still works under Node 24. If green, we know the 2026-06-02 cutover is risk-free for us.
2. Watch upstream: monitor dorny/paths-filter for a v4 (or v3.x patch) release that bumps runs.using to node24. When published, drop the env override and bump the pin.
3. Hard deadline: 2026-09-16 - must be on a Node 24 action by then or CI breaks.

## Fallback if upstream stalls

- Keep FORCE_JAVASCRIPT_ACTIONS_TO_NODE24=true permanently (officially supported by GitHub as the migration mechanism).
- Replace paths-filter with hand-rolled git diff --name-only logic in a shell step (loses caching of patterns; ~30 lines of YAML).
notes:
Resolved by bumping pin v3 -> v4 in commit d52a9f2 (2026-04-27).

Upstream had already shipped v4.0.0 on 2026-03-12 (1.5 months before our adoption of v3). The v4 tag changes nothing except 'runs.using: node20 -> node24'; diff confirmed against v3.0.3...v4.0.0. v4.0.1 (2026-03-17) adds merge-queue support which is irrelevant to our usage.

Verified by CI run 24981361010 on push event: Detect changed paths job log no longer contains the 'Node.js 20 actions are deprecated' warning. All five gated jobs passed.

No fallback measures needed: hard deadline (2026-09-16) is no longer relevant for paths-filter.
close_reason: Bumped to v4 in d52a9f2; CI verified clean of Node 20 warning.

title: Bump Swatinem/rust-cache pin when upstream lands warpbuild punycode fix
description:
Follow-up to nts-nvv. Filed upstream issue Swatinem/rust-cache#331 documenting the residual DEP0040 punycode warning that originates from @actions/warpbuild-cache@1.4.7's transitive node-fetch@2 → whatwg-url@5 → tr46@0.0.3 chain in v2.9.1.

When upstream releases a version that silences the warning (likely a v2.9.2+ that bumps @actions/warpbuild-cache or splits the bundle to lazy-load the warpbuild provider), bump the two pins in .github/workflows/ci.yml from v2.9.1 to the new tag and verify a fresh CI run no longer emits DEP0040.

If upstream is unresponsive for >3 months, reconsider local mitigation (NODE_OPTIONS=--no-deprecation on the cache step) or document acceptance.
notes:
## Upstream status check 2026-04-27

No action available. Upstream is unchanged since the issue was filed:

- Latest Swatinem/rust-cache release: still v2.9.1 (created 2026-03-12, predates the filing of #331 by ~6 weeks). No v2.9.2+ exists.
- Issue Swatinem/rust-cache#331: state open, updated_at equals created_at (2026-04-26T20:16:28Z) -- no maintainer or community response yet, no PR opened against it.

CI pin Swatinem/rust-cache@v2.9.1 (two occurrences in .github/workflows/ci.yml, lines 121 and 154) remains correct; no bump warranted.

Reverting status from in_progress back to open and continuing to wait. Per the original triage:
- 3-month deadline from filing (2026-04-26) is 2026-07-26.
- If still unresolved by then, reconsider local mitigation (NODE_OPTIONS=--no-deprecation on the cache step) or formally accept the warning as benign noise.

Impact remains stdout-only (no GitHub annotation, no exit-code change, no behavioural impact). P4.

title: Investigate residual DEP0040 punycode warning in Swatinem/rust-cache@v2.9.1
description:
`.github/workflows/ci.yml` pins `Swatinem/rust-cache` to `v2.9.1` (latest at time of pin, contains upstream PR #294 punycode fix), but DEP0040 stdout warnings still fire ~2x per `rust-bridge-sync` run on the harmonic CI (e.g. timestamps `12:44:42.5791914Z` and `12:46:21.8956491Z` in harmonic run `24931195614`). The same pin is in this repo's CI, so the same warnings will surface here once a few runs have accumulated.

Same Node process as `rust-cache`, so residual source is in v2.9.1's `dist/index.js`. Code search across the `rust-cache` repo returns 0 hits for `"punycode"` — residual is in a stripped/minified transitive (candidates: `@actions/glob`, `@actions/io`, `@actions/exec`, or a deeper Azure SDK dep that PR #294 missed).

Not blocking: stdout-only warning, never surfaced as a GitHub annotation (annotations_count remains 0).

## Investigation

1. Pull `v2.9.1` `dist/index.js` from the tag; grep for `whatwg-url`, `tr46`, `@azure/*` to find residual chain.
2. If found, file upstream issue against `Swatinem/rust-cache` with trace.
3. Watch for v2.9.2+ release that silences it; bump pin when available.

## Fallbacks if upstream unresponsive

- `NODE_OPTIONS=--no-deprecation` on the post-step (last resort, masks ALL deprecation warnings).
- Switch to `@actions/cache@v5` directly with manual key construction (loses rust-cache's smart caching).
acceptance_criteria: Residual punycode source identified and upstream issue filed (link captured), OR v2.9.2+ pin silences warning, OR decision documented to accept warning as benign noise.
notes:
## Investigation summary

Root cause traced: every punycode require chain in v2.9.1's bundled dist/restore/index.js originates from @actions/warpbuild-cache@1.4.7 (the github-actions.warp-cache npm alias added in PR #247 / v2.8.0). That package pulls in @azure/ms-rest-js@2.7.0 and @google-cloud/storage@7.19.0, both of which transitively depend on node-fetch@2.7.0 → whatwg-url@5.0.0 → tr46@0.0.3 → require("punycode").

PR #294 (shipped in v2.9.0/v2.9.1) fixed the @actions/cache chain via @azure/storage-blob@^12.29.1 but left the warpbuild chain untouched. Because @vercel/ncc bundles all three providers (default, buildjet, warpbuild) into a single dist file, the warpbuild module graph is evaluated on every action startup regardless of which provider the consumer selected.

## Bundle evidence

- L137429: `module.exports = require("punycode")` (module 24876)
- L135638: whatwg-url module (20905) requires it
- L111650: tr46 module (1552) requires it

## Resolution

Filed upstream issue Swatinem/rust-cache#331 with the full diagnostic, reverse dependency trace, and three suggested fix paths (bump warpbuild-cache, lazy-load the provider, or use package.json overrides).

Tracking bump of the v2.9.1 pin via follow-up nts-qc8.

## Impact assessment

Stdout-only warning. No GitHub annotation, no exit-code change, no behavioural impact. Cosmetic CI log noise only.
close_reason: Investigated and tracked upstream. Filed Swatinem/rust-cache#331 with full diagnostic. Pin bump tracked via follow-up nts-qc8.
